actions.thought: I am on the button with 7h 4s, which is a very weak hand. In a 3-handed game, while positional advantage is important, 74 offsuit is near the bottom of my range and generally does not warrant a raise or a call against unknown opponents. Folding is the most prudent play to start the tournament.

8-7 offsuit is a weak hand to play out of position against the Big Blind's range. Given the early stage of the tournament, it is better to preserve my stack and wait for a stronger starting hand than to play a marginal hand with a positional disadvantage.

I am on the button with 8d-Td, a strong suited connector that plays well in position. Opening the action with a raise is the standard aggressive play here to accumulate chips and capitalize on my positional advantage against the blinds. A raise size of 250 is optimal to balance pot size and fold equity.
79o is a very weak, unsuited hand. Facing a raise from the Button, playing from the Small Blind out of position with this holding is not profitable. The best and standard action here is to fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 4c 3s, which is an extremely weak hand. The Button (Flashlight) raised to 250, and the Small Blind folded. Although I am getting roughly 2.6:1 odds to call, playing 43o out of position against a button raise is generally a losing proposition long-term. I will fold and wait for a better spot.
With A8 offsuit on the button in a 3-handed game, this is a standard open. Raising to 2.5x the big blind applies pressure to the blinds and allows us to play in position if called.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Nine suited, a strong suited connector that plays well in multi-way or heads-up pots. The Button raised to 2.5x the big blind. Calling is a standard play here to see a flop and realize my equity without bloating the pot out of position early in the tournament.

With a pocket pair of fours in the big blind, I have favorable pot odds to call and see a flop. The pot is currently 600, and it costs me 150 to call. My primary goal is to set-mine cheaply in this multi-way pot. Raising is unnecessary here as I want to keep the field wide and the investment low while I hunt for a set.

I missed the flop completely with my Ten-Nine of hearts. The board contains a King, which is very much in the pre-flop raiser's range. Being out of position in a three-way pot, checking is the standard and most disciplined play to see how the action develops.

The flop is relatively dry for my pocket fours, though I do have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 3) and a backdoor flush draw. Given that I am facing two opponents, betting here would be an unnecessary bluff with low equity. Checking allows me to see the turn card for free and keeps the pot size manageable while I re-evaluate the strength of my hand against the field.

With Ad 8h on a 2c Ks 5c board in a multiway pot, I only have Ace-high and no club for a backdoor flush draw. Although the King hits my pre-flop raising range well, c-betting into two opponents on a fairly wet board (flush draw, some straight draws) without any equity to back it up is risky. Checking behind allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity, and take a free card to potentially hit an Ace.

The turn brings the Eight of Clubs, completing a potential flush and adding another low card to the board. I still have no pair and no real draw with Ten-Nine of hearts. Since everyone checked the flop, it's possible someone is trapping with a King or just checking back a weak hand. Given I have zero showdown value and no equity to continue, I will check again and fold if there is any significant bet.

The turn card (8c) is a flush-completing card, and I hold the 4c, giving me a weak flush draw to the 4. With three players in the pot and a board that has become significantly wetter, betting into two opponents with only a low pair and a weak draw is high-variance and likely unprofitable. Checking is the most prudent play to maintain pot control and see the river, keeping the pot size manageable while I evaluate my showdown value or potential to improve on the final card.

The turn brings an 8, giving me middle pair, but the board now has three clubs. I don't hold any clubs, so my hand is very vulnerable to flushes, and I am still behind any King. Since both opponents checked again, taking a free card to the river and controlling the pot size is the best option. Betting here would likely only get called by better hands or strong draws, and I would be in a tough spot if check-raised.

I have improved to a pair of Tens on the river. Since the flop and turn were checked through by all players, it is unlikely that anyone holds a monster hand like a slow-played flush or a strong King. I have moderate showdown value now. Checking is the safest option to realize this value; if I bet, I'm rarely called by worse hands and often folded out by better ones. If it's checked around, my pair of Tens has a good chance of winning the pot.

The board shows 2c-Ks-5c-8c-Td, and I hold 4c-4s. My pair of fours is extremely weak in this multi-way pot, as any pair on the board (2s, 5s, 8s, Ts, Ks) or any Ace-high beats me. Since the action has checked through the flop, turn, and river, it is highly likely that none of the players have a strong hand, but betting into two opponents with my weak holding would only be called by better hands. Checking is the most prudent strategy to reach showdown and avoid unnecessary losses.

The river brings a Ten. Both opponents checked for the third street in a row. I have third pair (8s) with an Ace kicker. Betting for value is too thin here, as it's unlikely a worse hand like a 5, 2, or Ace-high will call, while better hands like a 10, King, or a slowplayed flush might call. Checking behind to realize my showdown value is the safest play.

I am on the button with Ace-Eight of spades, which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. My goal is to maximize the value of my position. Raising here allows me to pick up the blinds immediately or play a pot in position against the small or big blind. I will raise to 250 chips, which is 2.5 times the big blind.

I am in the small blind with Ts-6c, which is a very weak hand. Playing this out of position against a button raise is mathematically disadvantageous and lacks the equity to warrant a call. I will fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

K3o is a very weak hand to defend from the big blind against a button raise. Out of position with a disconnected, unsuited weak King, folding is the standard, profitable play to avoid tough post-flop decisions.
4c7h is a very weak hand with no showdown value or playability. It is standard to fold junk hands like this, especially early in a tournament, to preserve my stack and wait for better opportunities.

With action folded to me in the Small Blind, 9s8s is a strong suited connector that plays very well heads-up. Raising to 3 big blinds takes the initiative, gives me fold equity pre-flop, and builds a pot when I have a hand that can easily flop strong draws or made hands.

I am in the Big Blind with 4s 2c, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. While ranges in blind-vs-blind battles are wider, 4-2 offsuit has very poor equity and playability post-flop. There is no reason to defend such a weak hand here, even with 2:1 pot odds, as I will frequently be dominated or forced to fold on most flops.

72 offsuit is the weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Being the first to act on the Button, attempting a steal with zero equity when called is unnecessarily risky. Folding is the only sensible play.

I am in the Small Blind with 8h 2h. The Button (Pro) has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Flashlight). While 82 suited has some post-flop potential, it is a very weak hand to play out of position against the Big Blind. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with J2o. While the button is the best position to attempt a steal, J2o is extremely weak and does not play well post-flop if called. Given the early stage of the tournament and the fact that I've already shown some aggression in previous hands, I will fold this hand and wait for a better spot to participate in a pot.

The button has folded, leaving me heads-up against the big blind. With 9d Td, I have a playable hand in the small blind. Completing the small blind (calling 50 more) is a standard, low-risk approach to see a flop given the pot odds, rather than risking a raise with a non-premium hand.

I have 2s 3c, which is one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Since I am in the Big Blind and the Small Blind has only limped, I have the option to check and see a free flop. Raising as a bluff with 3-high is unnecessary and risky, so checking to realize any miracle equity on the flop is the clear choice.

I have ten-high on a flop of 4-J-6. This board does not connect well with my holding of 9d Td, giving me no pair and no strong draws. As the small blind, acting first on the flop, checking is the most logical play to keep the pot small and avoid wasting chips on a bluff that has low likelihood of success against the big blind's range.
I have 3-high with no draw on a 4s Jh 6s flop. Flashlight checked to me, and there is absolutely no reason to bluff with zero equity. I will check behind to see a free turn card.

The board has paired the jack on the turn, which significantly reduces the likelihood that either player has improved significantly if they hadn't already hit the flop. With nine-high, I have zero showdown value and no meaningful draws. Checking is the most prudent strategy to keep the pot controlled and reach a cheap river, as betting here would be a pure bluff against an opponent who checked the flop, and would likely only be called by a range that beats me.

With 3-high and no draw on a paired board, I have zero showdown value and virtually no equity. Flashlight has checked again, but there is no compelling reason to bluff at this pot with the worst possible hand. I will check behind to see a free river card and keep the pot as small as possible.

I am on the river with ten-high. The board is 4s Jh 6s Jc 8s. There is no showdown value in my hand, and betting into the opponent after both players checked the previous two streets would essentially be a naked bluff. Given that the pot is small and there is a high likelihood the opponent has at least something to call with if I bet (like a jack, a pair of fours, or sixes), checking is the most rational decision to keep the pot small and reach a cheap showdown.

I have 3-high and play the board on the river (J-J-8-6-4). Since Flashlight has checked all three streets, they likely have a very weak hand, possibly A-high, K-high, or a low pair, but almost all of those hands still beat me. While I could try to bluff here, the pot is only 200 chips, and risking chips on a bluff in a limped pot with literally the bottom of my range isn't necessary. I will check behind and accept whatever the outcome is, either a chop if they also play the board or a loss to any high card.

4-5 offsuit is a weak starting hand with very little potential for strong board connection, even in a 3-handed game. It does not warrant an open-raise or a call in this position, so the best strategic decision is to fold.

With 9h8h in the Small Blind and the Button folded, I have a strong suited connector that plays very well both as a steal and post-flop. I'll raise to 3 big blinds to take the initiative and potentially win the pot right here, while having good equity if the Big Blind calls.

I am holding AQo in the Big Blind against a raise from the Small Blind. In a three-handed game, this is a premium hand. Furthermore, I will have position on the Small Blind for the rest of the hand. I am choosing to 3-bet for value and to take initiative, as my hand is significantly ahead of a typical Small Blind opening range. A standard raise to 900 chips is appropriate here.

I opened with 9h8h from the Small Blind to steal, but the Big Blind 3-bet to 9 big blinds. Playing a suited connector out of position against a strong 3-bet range is unprofitable, as I will struggle to realize my equity and could face reverse implied odds. Folding is the disciplined and correct GTO play here.
I am on the Button with Ten-Three offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. I will fold pre-flop to avoid playing a marginal hand out of position post-flop or facing a 3-bet.

I am in the Small Blind with Q9o. The button has folded, so it is just me and the Big Blind. Q9o is a strong enough hand to open for a raise in a blind-versus-blind situation to try and take down the pot immediately or play with the initiative. I will raise to 3 big blinds (300).

King-Jack suited is a very strong hand in a Small Blind versus Big Blind scenario. Since Flash has been playing aggressively, I prefer to keep the pot manageable and use my positional advantage post-flop rather than bloating the pot with a 3-bet. Calling is the standard and most profitable defense here, allowing me to see a flop and evaluate the situation further.

The flop of 5d-Kh-5h is favorable for a continuation bet from the pre-flop aggressor. Although I have missed the flop with Q9o, the presence of the King and the paired board makes it difficult for my opponent to continue without a King or a Five. I will bet approximately 40% of the pot to represent strength and try to take the pot down immediately.

Top pair with a Jack kicker is a strong holding on this board. Flash has demonstrated aggression, so this bet could easily be a continuation bet with air or a weaker King. By calling, I keep his bluffs in the pot and maintain a manageable stack, avoiding bloating the pot against potential traps while still extracting value from weaker holdings.

The Jack of Diamonds on the turn does not improve my hand, and after my flop continuation bet was called, it is likely that the opponent has a piece of the board, such as a King, a Five, or a flush draw. Continuing to bluff into a range that called on a paired board is risky. I will check to see how the opponent reacts, potentially taking a free card if they check behind, or folding if they face me with a significant bet.

Flash has checked the turn on a board of 5d-Kh-5h-Jd. With my two pair (Kings and Jacks), I have a very strong hand. Flash's check indicates weakness, or potentially a trap, but I cannot afford to give a free card when I have such a strong holding. Betting now allows me to extract value from weaker Kings or medium pairs and helps me define the strength of his hand. I will bet 600 into the 1100 pot to build the pot in my favor while still keeping the price reasonable.

I started this hand as the pre-flop aggressor and followed up with a continuation bet on the flop, which was called. After checking the turn Jack of Diamonds, Flashlight has bet 600 into the pot. My Queen-high has no real equity or showdown value against a range that called a flop bet on a paired board and is now leading on the turn. Attempting a bluff-check-raise is too risky here, as Flashlight could easily have a King, a Five, or a better Jack. The most disciplined play is to fold and preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 62 suited. While three-handed play allows for a wider opening range, 62s is significantly below the threshold of a profitable opening hand from the button, especially against opponents who have shown willingness to defend or 3-bet. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the small blind with a very weak hand (23 offsuit). Since the button folded, I am facing the big blind. Calling would require putting in another 50 chips, but playing such a low-equity hand OOP is not advisable. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to conserve my stack.

I am on the button with Ace-Eight offsuit, which is a strong starting hand in this position. Raising allows me to take control of the hand, steal the blinds, or play in a favorable position post-flop. A raise to 300 is a standard open given the pot size.

Facing a 3bb open from the Button while in the Small Blind with Pocket Deuces (22). Calling here to set-mine out of position is marginal at best, as we will miss the flop 88% of the time and struggle to realize our equity. Furthermore, the Big Blind is still left to act and could squeeze, forcing us to fold our equity. The most disciplined and EV-sound play is to fold the bottom pair from the Small Blind against a standard raise.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Six suited. Flashlight has raised to 300 from the Button, and the Small Blind has folded. I need to call 200 more into a total pot of 450, giving me pot odds of approximately 2.25:1. In a 3-handed game, a Button opening range is typically very wide, and Queen-Six suited has enough equity and playability post-flop to justify a defense, even though I will be out of position. Folding is too conservative, and raising is unnecessary given the strength of my hand and the position. I will call to see a flop.

The flop of Ad-Kd-2d is extremely strong for the pre-flop raiser's range (Flashlight). Having missed the board entirely with my Queen-high and no significant draw, I have no reason to lead into the aggressor. I will check and likely fold to any significant bet, as my hand has very little equity here.

I flopped top pair with an Ace on an Ace-King-Deuce board. Since the opponent checked to me and I am in position, a continuation bet is standard to extract value from weaker hands like lower Aces or kings, and to charge potential draws. A bet of 350 represents just over half the pot, putting pressure on the opponent while maintaining control of the hand.

The flop of 2d-Ac-Kd is extremely unfavorable for my hand (Qc6c). I have no pair, no draw, and don't even hold a diamond on a monotone board. Flashlight, as the pre-flop aggressor from the button, has many Aces, Kings, and flush combinations in their range. Given I have zero equity and am out of position, I will fold to the continuation bet to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 6s7h in a 3-handed game. Offsuit connectors like 67o are generally too weak to open, even with the positional advantage. Folding is the most profitable decision here to conserve my stack for better starting hands.

I am in the Small Blind with KJs, which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game, especially when the button has folded. Standard strategy dictates a raise here to either win the blinds immediately or play a pot with a range advantage against the Big Blind. I will raise to 300.

I have pocket eights, which is a strong hand heads-up against a Small Blind raise. Since I have position post-flop, 3-betting is the optimal play to take the initiative, build the pot, and capitalize on my equity advantage. A raise to 900 serves as a standard 3-bet sizing to force folds or get value from weaker holdings.

I have KJs in the Small Blind, a very strong hand 3-handed. After my initial raise to 300, Flashlight 3-bet to 900 from the Big Blind. While being out of position is a disadvantage, KJs has excellent playability and sufficient equity against a standard 3-betting range to justify a call. Folding is too conservative, and 4-betting might overplay the hand. Calling allows me to see a flop and utilize my hand's post-flop potential.

I am out of position in a 3-bet pot against the pre-flop aggressor. Although I have a gutshot straight draw and one overcard, I currently only have King-high. Leading into the aggressor on this board is generally ill-advised. I will check to see how the Big Blind proceeds and evaluate based on their bet sizing.

The board texture is fairly coordinated, containing straight and flush draws. My pocket eights are now an underpair to the 9s and Tc on the board. Since I am the pre-flop aggressor and in position, checking back is a standard play here to control the size of the pot and realize my equity without bloating the pot against a range that likely hit this board well. Betting would likely force me into a tough spot if my opponent check-raises, so checking is the safer, more strategic play.

On the turn, the board pairs with a nine. Flashlight checked back on the flop after 3-betting pre-flop, which suggests he might be holding Ace-high (like AK or AQ), a mid-pocket pair, or is simply playing cautiously on a semi-connected board. I have King-high with a gutshot straight draw (Queen). Since Flashlight checked the flop, I have the opportunity to see a free river card to potentially hit my straight or an overcard. Checking here is the most prudent move to realize my equity without bloating the pot with just King-high.

I am in position with pocket eights on a board of 9s-5s-Tc-9h. The pair of nines on the board makes my pocket eights essentially a bluff-catcher. Flash has checked to me twice, which signals weakness or a trap. Betting here turns my hand into a bluff and would likely only be called by hands that beat me (any nine, ten, or pocket pair higher than eights) while causing folds from hands I currently beat (ace-high, low straight draws). Checking back is the most optimal play as it allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity, and keep the opponent's range wide, potentially inducing a bluff on the river.

On the river, the board is 9s-5s-Tc-9h-2d. I have King-high (Kc Jc). The pre-flop aggressor (Flashlight) 3-bet pre-flop but has checked both the flop and the turn. This passive behavior suggests either a marginal hand like Ace-high or a medium pocket pair that is wary of the board, or a complete air hand. While a bet might occasionally force a fold from better Ace-high hands, checking is the most prudent play. If Flashlight checks back, I have a small chance of winning at showdown against some missed draws or worse King-highs, though I likely lose to his range. If he bets, I will fold as King-high has no real value in a 3-bet pot facing a river bet.

The board is 9s-5s-Tc-9h-2d. My pocket eights have showdown value, but are unlikely to be called by worse hands if I bet, and I would be folding out hands I currently beat (like missed straight draws or lower pairs). Since the opponent checked, betting turns my hand into a bluff unnecessarily. Checking back allows me to take my showdown value to the end and avoid bloating the pot against a range that may have connected with the board. It is the safest and most strategic play.

I am on the button with Ace-Five offsuit, which is a standard opening hand in a three-handed game. Opening here allows me to take control of the pot and utilize my position throughout the hand. A 3x raise is a standard sizing to apply pressure on the blinds.

My hand, 6d 3c, is extremely weak and has very poor equity, especially playing out of position against an opening raise from the button. There is no strategic value in defending this hand, so folding is the correct disciplined play to preserve my stack.

Facing a 3x raise from the Button, A4 offsuit is too weak to call out of position. While it can sometimes be used as a 3-bet bluff, folding is the standard and safest play to preserve our stack for better opportunities.

Holding 5-2 offsuit is among the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em with no playability or high-card strength. Despite being on the button, there is no strategic justification for entering the pot with this hand. Folding is the correct disciplined play to preserve my stack and wait for a more profitable opportunity.

I am in the Small Blind with 93o, which is a very weak, unplayable hand. Since the Button folded, I could try to steal, but 93o is too weak even for a steal, especially out of position against the Big Blind. The best action is to simply fold.

AJs is a premium suited broadway hand. Being on the button, we have a positional advantage. A standard opening raise to 3BB (300) will thin the field, build the pot, and give us the initiative going into the flop.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Deuce suited. The Button (Pro) has opened for 3x the big blind. While King-Deuce suited has some value in 3-handed play, calling from the Small Blind puts me out of position for the rest of the hand against the initial raiser and potentially the Big Blind. Given the weak kicker and the disadvantageous position, folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Eight offsuit, facing a standard 3x raise from the button. Given the pot odds (I need to call 200 into a 450 pot), this is a standard defensive play. K8o is sufficient to defend the blind against a wide button opening range, and I have the stack depth to see a flop.

I am in the Big Blind with King-high on an Ace-high board. I have no pair and no meaningful draw. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser, the standard and correct play is to check. I will be looking to fold to any continuation bet as this board hits the raiser's range significantly harder than mine.

With Top Pair, Jack Kicker on an Ad 9h 6d board, I have a strong but vulnerable hand. The board has a flush draw (diamonds) and some straight draws. Betting for value and protection is appropriate. Flashlight checked to me, and a continuation bet of around half the pot (350) will extract value from weaker aces, middle pairs, and draws while keeping the initiative.

I am in the Big Blind with King-high on an Ace-high board (Ad-9h-6d). I have no pair, no legitimate draws, and I am out of position against the button raiser. An Ace hits the raiser's range very hard. Calling 350 into a 1000 pot with essentially 0% equity is a losing play. The disciplined decision is to fold and preserve my stack.

K5o is a marginal hand for an opening raise from the Button in 3-handed play. While often included in wide opening ranges, my opponents have shown a willingness to defend and 3-bet. Given the current stack sizes and my desire to maintain a disciplined image, I will fold this hand and wait for a stronger opening opportunity.

My hand (9-3 offsuit) is among the weakest possible holdings in Texas Hold'em. With no prospects for making a strong hand and poor playability, I should not invest any additional chips into the pot. Folding is the disciplined and optimal decision.

With Ace-Jack offsuit on the button, I hold a premium hand in this 3-handed scenario. Opening with a raise is the standard, aggressive play to maximize value and potentially steal the blinds immediately. A raise to 300 is a solid opening size to put pressure on the blinds.
J7o is a weak offsuit hand. Facing a 3x open raise from the button, playing this hand out of position from the small blind is a clear fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 9h 2h. Flashlight has raised to 300 from the Button, and the Small Blind has folded. While I am getting decent pot odds to call (200 chips to win a 450 chip pot), 92s is an extremely weak hand to play out of position for the rest of the hand. My stack is healthy at 9000 chips, and there is no need to defend with such a marginal hand against a button open this early in the tournament.
KQs is a very strong hand 3-handed, especially on the Button. I am opening with a standard raise to 2.5x the big blind to build the pot and take control of the hand.

I am in the Small Blind with K9o. The Button (Pro) has opened for 2.5x (250). K9o is a solid hand to defend with against a button raise, especially with a 2.5x sizing. While playing out of position is not ideal, folding would be too tight and 3-betting might turn a hand with decent showdown value into a bluff. I will call the 200 to see a flop and evaluate, keeping an eye on the Big Blind who still has to act.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Eight suited. The Button opened, and the Small Blind called. I am getting excellent pot odds (150 to win 600) to complete my bet. Ace-Eight suited is a strong enough hand to see a flop multi-way, and there is no reason to fold or gamble with a 3-bet here. Calling is the most disciplined and profitable play.

On a paired board of 7d-7s-5h, I am first to act. With King high (Kd-9c), I have some showdown value but don't want to build a pot out of position against two opponents. The standard play here is to check to the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) and see how the action develops. This board is relatively dry, and checking allows me to control the pot size and potentially see a free turn card if everyone else checks through.

The board is paired (7-7-5) and quite dry, which is generally not a great texture for betting into two opponents with only Ace-high. Checking allows me to control the pot size and see how the original raiser (Pro) proceeds. Betting would be overplaying my hand and likely result in folding out hands I might beat or isolating myself against a better hand.

With King-Queen high on a dry paired board (7-7-5) after both opponents have checked, I have a good opportunity to make a continuation bet. This board favors my pre-flop raising range, and a small c-bet (around 1/3 pot) can often fold out random overcards, weak floats, or small pocket pairs. I also retain equity with my two strong overcards if I get called.

On a paired flop of 7d-7s-5h in a three-way pot, the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) has placed a continuation bet of 250 into a 750 pot. While I am getting 5:1 pot odds to call with my King-high (Kd-9c), I am out of position against two players. My hand has very little equity if I am behind, and the presence of the Big Blind (Flashlight) still to act behind me makes calling risky. If I call and Flashlight also calls or raises, I am forced to play a bloated pot out of position with a marginal hand. Given that I have no piece of this board and only overcards that might not even be good if they hit, the most disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

The board is paired with sevens and my hand is just Ace-high with no draw. Pro made a continuation bet into the pot, and while the sizing is relatively small, continuing without a pair or a strong draw on this board texture is generally unprofitable and leads to difficult spots on later streets. I will fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

I am on the Button with A4o in a three-handed game. An Ace-high hand is well within the standard opening range for this position. Raising allows me to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds immediately. A 3x raise is a standard size to apply pressure on the blinds.

I am in the small blind with Qc 6h, a weak hand that is significantly dominated by standard button opening ranges. Playing out of position with such a weak holding is a recipe for trouble, and I do not have a strong incentive to defend this specific hand against Flash's opening raise. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop, and 3-betting as a bluff is suboptimal here. A disciplined fold is the most prudent decision to preserve my stack.
I am in the Big Blind facing a 3x open from the Button. My hand, 9d4c, is offsuit, disconnected, and extremely weak. It has very little equity or playability post-flop, making it a clear fold.

Pocket sixes are a strong starting hand, especially in position on the button. With effective stacks healthy, opening for 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard move to take control of the hand pre-flop, isolate opponents, or capitalize on position post-flop.

I am in the Small Blind with T3s. Flashlight (Button) raised to 300. Ten-Three suited is a very weak starting hand, and playing it out of position against a raise is not profitable. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

In a 3-handed game, K8o is a solid hand to defend the Big Blind against a button raise. Flashlight's raise to 300 is standard, and I need to call 200 more into a pot of 450, giving me pot odds of 3.25:1. This is a clear call to see a flop, as K8o has sufficient equity against a typical button opening range, even though I will be out of position post-flop.

I have flopped top two pair (Kings and Eights), which is an extremely strong hand in a three-handed game. The board contains a flush draw (diamonds), so I need to be mindful of that. As the Big Blind, checking to the pre-flop aggressor (Flashlight) is the standard play to see if they will follow through with a continuation bet. This allows me to either check-call to keep their bluffs in or check-raise to build value against a King with a worse kicker or a diamond draw.

With a low pair of sixes on a King-high board, my hand is currently weak and vulnerable to almost any turn card.
