Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the big blind with a King-high hand (K-2 offsuit). Pro raised to 250 from the button, and I need to call 150 to see the flop in a pot that is laying decent odds. Given my chip leader status and the nature of short-handed play, I should defend my big blind against a standard raise from the button. Folding would be too tight, so I will call the bet.

The flop is Td-Qh-3c, and I hold Kc-2d for King-high. I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser. With no significant equity, a check is the most standard and defensive play to evaluate the opponent's action before making further decisions.

With pocket sevens on a Queen-Ten-Three board, I have third pair. This board hits the Big Blind's defending range fairly well (hands like QJ, QT, JT, T9, etc.). Betting here is unlikely to get better hands to fold and will mostly just get called or raised by hands that beat us. Checking behind allows me to control the pot size and evaluate on the turn, while keeping my marginal showdown value intact.

I am in the Big Blind with King-high (K-c, 2-d) on a board of Td-Qh-3c-Qd. The pre-flop raiser (Pro) checked back on the flop, which suggests weakness or a desire to control the pot. The turn is the Queen of Diamonds, which pairs the board and likely improves the range of the pre-flop raiser. My hand has no showdown value, and betting into the pre-flop aggressor is unlikely to force a fold often enough to be profitable, especially since the Queen pairs the board. Checking is the most prudent play to maintain pot control and evaluate the opponent's intentions.

With the board pairing the Queen on the turn and the opponent checking again, our pocket sevens remain a marginal made hand with some showdown value. Betting now won't get better hands like a Ten or a higher pocket pair to fold, and worse hands will likely fold. Checking allows us to maintain pot control, keep the opponent's bluffing range wide for the river, and potentially get to a cheap showdown.

I am in the big blind with King-high (Kc-2d) on a board of Td-Qh-3c-Qd-8h. The board is paired and does not contain any of my cards, leaving me with only King-high. My opponent (Pro) checked both the flop and the turn, suggesting they likely have a marginal hand or are also giving up on the pot. Betting with King-high is a low-percentage bluff that is unlikely to force a fold from any hand that beats me, and calling a bet would be impossible. Checking is the standard, prudent play to reach showdown or concede the pot if the opponent bets.

The river brings an 8. The board is T-Q-3-Q-8. My pocket sevens are likely good against missed draws and Ace-high hands. However, a bet here is unlikely to get called by worse hands, and better hands (like an 8, a 10, or 99) probably won't fold. Checking behind is the best play to realize our showdown value without risking more chips.

I am on the button with Ace-six offsuit. In a three-handed game, this is a standard opening hand. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds or play the hand in position.

I am in the small blind with King-Eight offsuit, which is a relatively weak hand to defend from out of position against a button raise. Given the aggressive nature of the opponent (Flash) and the fact that I would be playing the rest of the hand out of position, the best strategic decision is to fold and preserve my chip stack for a better spot.

T7s is a marginal suited two-gapper. Facing a 3x open from the Button while out of position, it's generally too weak to profitably flat-call, as we will struggle to realize our equity post-flop. 3-betting as a bluff is an option, but with better candidates available, folding is the most disciplined and standard play.

I am dealt pocket sixes on the button, which is a strong position to play this hand. Pocket pairs have good equity and play well in a raised pot, especially when holding the initiative. I will open to 300 to take control of the hand and put pressure on the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with 7h 4s, which is a very weak and unconnected offsuit hand. Facing a raise to 300 from the Button, the most profitable decision is to simply fold and save my chips for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Six offsuit. The Button (Flashlight) has raised to 3 big blinds. While the pot odds are roughly 2.25 to 1, J6o is a very weak hand to defend out of position. Given the stack sizes and the tournament context, it's better to fold and wait for a more favorable hand or position than to play a marginal hand out of position against a stack that covers me.
86o is a weak offsuit gapper. Even from the Button, it is too weak to open-raise or call. I will fold and wait for a better starting hand.

I am holding Ace-King offsuit (AKo) in the Small Blind. This is a premium hand in a 3-handed game. With the button (Pro) folding, I am head-to-head with the Big Blind (Flashlight). I want to raise for value and to take control of the pot. A standard raise to 300 is consistent with my previous betting patterns and puts pressure on the Big Blind.

I am in the Big Blind with 9c2c. While the pot odds are attractive (calling 200 to win a pot of 400), 9-2 suited is a very weak hand to play out of position against an aggressive opponent like Flash. Folding is the most disciplined and long-term profitable decision here.

I am on the button with Ace-Deuce offsuit in a 3-handed game. This is a standard opening hand to put pressure on the blinds. My stack size of 54 BBs allows for a standard open. I will raise to 300, which has been my consistent opening size in previous hands, to try and take down the blinds or play the hand in position.

I am in the Small Blind with K4 offsuit. This is a weak hand to defend with, especially out of position against a button raise. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop with limited board coverage. Given the stack depths and the fact that the Big Blind is still to act, the most disciplined and optimal play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

We are in the Big Blind with 7d 5d, a suited one-gapper. The Button (Flash) raises to 3x. Getting decent pot odds (200 more to win 450) and closing the action, this is a standard defend. We have a hand that can flop well in terms of straights and flushes, and we can easily check-fold if we miss.

I flopped an open-ended straight draw (any 4 or 9 makes a straight) along with a backdoor diamond draw on a wet T-8-6 board. Out of position against the pre-flop raiser, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. I will evaluate his c-bet sizing and likely call to see the turn, or potentially check-raise as a semi-bluff given my strong equity.

On this connected board of Ten of Spades, Eight of Spades, and Six of Diamonds, my Ace-Deuce offsuit has very little equity. This board texture favors the Big Blind's defending range, which contains many middling connectors and suited hands. Since Pro checked, I have the option to check back to see a free turn card. Betting into a board that hits the opponent's range so well is risky, as I'm unlikely to get folds from any pair or draw. Checking allows me to keep the pot small and potentially catch an Ace on the turn or evaluate based on the next card.

The turn brings the Queen of Spades, putting three spades on the board. I only have an open-ended straight draw with no spades, so my draw is severely devalued, as hitting a straight might still lose to a flush. Since my opponent checked behind on the flop, they could have a marginal made hand or a draw, including spade draws that just got there. Being out of position with a weak hand and weak draw on a mono-tone runout, the best option is to check and likely fold to a bet.

On this connected and flush-heavy board, my Ace-Deuce offsuit has very little equity. Pro's second check suggests weakness or a marginal hand, but betting into this texture after checking the flop is unlikely to generate enough folds from better hands like pairs or draws. Checking back allows me to see the river for free and potentially win at showdown if Pro has complete air, while keeping the pot small with my weak holding.

The river brings a fourth spade to the board, giving me only a bottom pair of fives with no flush. Given that the board is a 4-flush, my hand has virtually no showdown value. Attempting to bluff here is risky, as Flash's flop and turn checks could easily include hands with a single spade that he would now use to call a bet. Checking and giving up if faced with a bet is the safest and most standard line.

On a four-flush board of spades, I hold the Ace of Clubs and the Deuce of Spades. While I have made a flush, the Deuce of Spades is the lowest possible spade, meaning any single spade in Pro's hand higher than a two beats me. Pro has checked through all three streets (flop, turn, and river), which indicates significant weakness or a marginal hand. However, betting here is unlikely to get value from hands worse than a low flush, as most non-spade hands will fold, and any spade higher than a two will likely call or raise. Checking back allows me to see the showdown and potentially win against hands with no spades while losing the minimum if Pro is slow-playing a higher spade or simply has a small one.

I am on the button with a King-high hand, which is a strong position to open the action. A standard raise of 300 chips is appropriate to attempt to steal the blinds and take the initiative in the hand, leveraging my position and healthy stack size.

With Q5o in the Small Blind facing a Button raise, the hand is far too weak to defend. Playing a marginal, unsuited, disconnected hand out of position is a long-term losing strategy. Folding is the standard and correct play.

In the big blind, I am facing a button raise to 300. With [Js, 9d], I have a hand that is strong enough to defend against a standard button opening range, especially given the pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 650). Folding would be too tight, and 3-betting is unnecessary with this hand strength. I will call to see the flop.

I have middle pair (Jacks) on an Ace-high board with a potential club flush draw. In this situation, checking to the pre-flop aggressor (Flashlight) is the standard and most prudent play. My hand is strong enough to call a continuation bet depending on the sizing, but leading out (donk betting) would likely only get called or raised by hands that beat me (like an Ace) and fold out hands I currently dominate.

I am on the button with King-high on an Ace-high board (Jc-7s-Ac). Since the Big Blind checked to me, I have the opportunity to take a free card or check behind to control the pot size. Betting here with air against a range that includes many Aces is unlikely to generate folds and forces me to play a bloated pot with a weak hand. Checking behind is the most disciplined and strategic play to realize my potential or minimize losses.

On the turn 8s, the board is now Ac Jc 7s 8s. Having checked the flop and seen Flashlight check behind, it's less likely they have a strong Ace. My pair of Jacks (Js 9d) remains a solid medium-strength hand. I will check again to see if Flashlight wants to bet a draw or air, or to simply take my hand to showdown as cheaply as possible. Leading out here might force out hands I beat and get called by better ones.

On the turn, the board is Jc-7s-Ac-8s. I am sitting on the button with King-high. Having checked the flop, my hand range looks capped, and the current board texture does not favor a bluff. Betting now would likely only be called by better hands or hands with significant equity against me. Checking behind allows me to control the pot size and reach a cheap showdown, maintaining discipline against an opponent who might be trapping or holding a marginal made hand.

I have middle pair (Jacks) on a board with an overcard (Ace). The pre-flop aggressor (Flashlight) checked both the flop and the turn, which suggests they likely do not have a strong Ace or a set. However, the river card (6c) completes a potential club flush and some straight possibilities (though unlikely). Since my hand has good showdown value but is unlikely to get called by many worse hands if I bet, checking is the most prudent play to reach showdown and realize my equity.

I hold a pair of sixes on a board of Jc-7s-Ac-8s-6c. My opponent has checked three consecutive streets, indicating weakness. While my pair of sixes is a made hand, it is very low in the range relative to the board, which contains an Ace, Jack, and Eight. Betting here is unlikely to get value from worse hands (as the opponent likely has nothing or a lower pair that they might check-fold, but betting into them only risks paying off if I am wrong or getting check-raised). Checking behind is the most disciplined approach, allowing me to take the showdown and avoid bloating the pot unnecessarily with a marginal holding.

J7o is a weak offsuit hand. Being first to act on the button in a 3-handed game, this hand does not have enough equity or playability to justify a raise or a call. Folding is the standard and correct play to preserve our stack.

I have KTo in the Small Blind. With the Button folding, it is a blind-vs-blind situation. KTo is a significantly above-average hand in this spot. I will raise to 300 to take the initiative and attempt to win the blinds or play a heads-up pot with a range advantage.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ace-Five offsuit. The Small Blind has raised to 300, making the pot 400 and requiring me to call 200 more. A5o is a playable hand in a blind-versus-blind scenario, and calling is a standard defensive play to see a flop given my stack advantage and position advantage for the post-flop streets.

I raised pre-flop and have the range advantage on this dry J-high board. King-high still has some value, but a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) is a standard play here to maintain initiative and potentially fold out air or weak hands from Flashlight's calling range.

I have flopped bottom pair with an Ace kicker on a relatively dry board (3-5-J). The opponent's bet represents a standard continuation bet. Folding is too tight given the board texture, and raising would likely only be called by better hands (like a Jack) or induce a fold from hands I am currently beating. Calling is the most balanced approach to control the pot size and see the turn.

On the turn 9h, the board becomes more coordinated, potentially completing straight draws or offering heart flush draws. Having been called on the flop, it is likely Flashlight has a piece of the board (like a Jack or a mid-pair) or a draw. My King-high has limited showdown value, and firing a second barrel is unlikely to fold out a Jack or a hand that improved with the 9. I will check to control the pot and see how Flashlight responds, potentially realizing my equity for free if they check back.

I have bottom pair (fives) with an Ace kicker on a board of 3d-5s-Jh-9h. Flash has checked to me on the turn. My hand is a marginal showdown value hand and is likely behind any pocket pair, Jack, or nine. Betting here is risky as it will likely only be called by better hands or induce a check-raise, while folding out hands that I am currently beating. Checking back allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity, and see the river for free to potentially improve my hand or take a cheap showdown.

I have King-high on a board that paired the nine on the river (3d-5s-Jh-9h-9s). My pre-flop raise and flop continuation bet were called by Flashlight, but the turn went check-check. This suggests Flashlight likely has some showdown value, such as a weak Jack, a small pair (22-88), or perhaps a 5. While the nine pairing on the river is an interesting card, betting now would essentially be a bluff to try and fold out a small pair. However, since the turn was checked through, it's unlikely I can represent a very strong hand convincingly. Checking is the most prudent play to see if I can get to a cheap showdown or fold if Flashlight bets, as King-high rarely wins if the opponent commits more chips to the pot on the river.

I have bottom pair (fives) with an Ace kicker on a board of 3d-5s-Jh-9h-9s. Flash has checked to me on the river. My hand has some showdown value against complete bluffs but loses to any pair of Jacks, Nines, or even a better five with a better kicker. Betting for value is unlikely to get called by worse hands, and it would only bloat a pot against hands that have me beat. Checking back is the most optimal play to reach showdown and minimize losses against a potential trap or better hand.

I am on the button with J5o. While 3-handed play warrants a wider opening range, J5o is generally considered too weak to open under standard conditions. With 55 big blinds, I have enough of a stack to remain patient and wait for a more profitable opening hand rather than attempting a steal with a sub-optimal holding.

I am in the Small Blind with a strong hand, Ace-high suited (As3s), against the Big Blind after the Button folded. Blind-vs-blind situations often allow for aggressive play to steal the blinds or take the lead. Raising to 300, which is three times the big blind, is a standard and effective move to put pressure on my opponent and potentially win the pot immediately or control the hand with the initiative.

JTo is a reasonable hand to defend blind versus blind against a 3x open from the Small Blind. I have positional advantage post-flop and decent equity against a wide stealing range, making a call profitable here.

I am the pre-flop raiser and have the initiative. The flop of 8c-2c-5s is relatively dry and favors my range as the aggressor. Although I only have Ace-high, I have a backdoor flush draw and an overcard. My opponent has shown a high tendency to play passively in previous hands, so a continuation bet of half the pot (300) should be sufficient to either take down the pot immediately or define my opponent's range.

My hand is Jack-high with no direct pair or strong draw on an 8c-2c-5s board. Facing a half-pot continuation bet from the Small Blind, I do not have enough equity or strong backdoor potential to justify a call or a bluff raise. Folding is the most straightforward and profitable decision here to preserve my stack.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game, which is a prime position to open and attempt to steal the blinds. Although 4h-Th is a mediocre starting hand, it is suited and holds reasonable playability in position. Given my stack size and previous history of opening standard amounts (300) from the button, this is a profitable play to maintain aggression and apply pressure to the blinds.
My hand is Ten-Three offsuit, which is a bottom-tier, disconnected starting hand. Facing a 3x raise from the Button and being out of position in the Small Blind, this is a clear and immediate fold.

I am in the Big Blind holding 4c 2s, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Flashlight, the chip leader on the button, has raised to 300. Defending my blind with such a poor hand out of position is not profitable, especially given that I have a shorter stack than my opponents. I will fold and wait for a better spot to commit chips.

With Ace-Six offsuit on the button, I have a decent hand to attempt a blind steal. A standard raise to 2.5x the big blind (250) applies pressure on the blinds and often wins the pot uncontested. If I face significant resistance (like a 3-bet), I can easily fold this marginal holding.

Pro is opening from the button, which typically signifies a wide range in a 3-handed game. Queen-Ten offsuit is a strong hand in this context, well ahead of many button-opening hands. While 3-betting is a viable option to seize the initiative and potentially fold out the button, calling allows me to see a flop and realize my equity without overcommitting chips out of position, especially with the chip leader yet to act in the big blind. The pot odds of 2:1 make this a standard defensive call in the small blind.

I am in the Big Blind with 5s-8c, a very weak hand. Even though I am getting decent pot odds (150 to win a pot of 600, requiring 20% equity), this hand has very poor playability, especially against two opponents. It is better to conserve my stack and wait for a more advantageous situation.

I have top pair (Tens) with a Queen kicker on a paired board of 8-8-10. This is a very strong hand in a 3-handed game. Typically, on a paired board like this, checking to the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) is the best play. It allows them to continue with their bluffs or bet with weaker hands that they might fold if I were to lead out. While an 8 is possible in Pro's range, I am currently ahead of most of their button-opening range. I will check and plan to call a standard continuation bet.

The board is paired and coordinated with spades, giving my opponent a lot of potential drawing hands or pairs in their pre-flop calling range. With just Ace-high and no real draws, I don't have enough equity to justify a continuation bet, and a c-bet might get called by better hands or draws. Checking behind allows me to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the turn while potentially keeping the lead against weaker high-card hands.

With the board now showing 8-8-10-8, I have a full house (Eights full of Tens). This is an extremely strong hand in this spot. The only hands that beat me are pocket pairs JJ, QQ (though I hold a Queen), KK, AA, or the case 8 (8c). Since Pro checked the flop, it is less likely they have an 8 or a high pocket pair, as they would typically bet those for value or protection on a 10-high board. By checking the turn, I am hoping to induce a bluff from Pro's air or a bet from an Ace-high hand that might think their kicker is relevant or that they can take the pot away. If I bet here, I likely only get called by hands that beat me or other Tens for a chop.

With the turn bringing a third eight to the board, my Ace-high is currently strong as it plays as trip eights with an Ace-Ten kicker. However, my hand is extremely vulnerable: because the board has three of a kind, any river card that pairs my opponent's hole cards will give them a full house, easily beating my trips. By betting here, I deny equity to his random broadway or suited connector hands that missed the flop but could randomly pair up on the river to beat me. A sizing of around 40% of the pot should be enough to fold out his King-high or Queen-high hands while getting value/protection.

I have a full house (Eights full of Tens) on a board of 8-8-T-8. This is an incredibly strong hand, as it beats all of Pro's range except for the case eight (8c), or pocket pairs JJ, KK, and AA (and QQ, which I block). Since Pro checked the flop, those high pocket pairs are less likely. My hand beats all other Tens (like AT or KT) because I make the second pair for the full house, whereas they would only have three-of-a-kind. I also beat pocket pairs like 99 or 77 and all bluffs. I am raising for value to build the pot against a Ten or a stubborn pocket pair that might believe I am bluffing the triple-paired board.

My opponent's check-raise on the turn is extremely strong. On a board with three eights and a ten, any pocket pair or any ten in his hand makes a full house, and an eight makes quads. I only have Ace-high, which loses to all of those value hands. Even though I have the best possible kicker to the trips on board, it's highly unlikely he is check-raise bluffing with a worse high-card hand. Folding is the only sensible option here.

I am on the button with a suited Jack. In a three-handed game, it's standard to open a wide range from the button to put pressure on the blinds. My stack is the shortest at the table, but still healthy at 60 big blinds. I will maintain my aggressive opening strategy with a standard 3x raise.

I am in the small blind with a weak offsuit hand (T9o) against a button open. Being out of position with a marginal holding against the button is a losing proposition, and I do not need to force the action given my large chip lead. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my stack.

K2s is a weak suited king that plays very poorly out of position. Facing a 3x open from the button, calling would leave me out of position in a bloated pot with a hand that often faces reverse implied odds when hitting top pair. Folding is the disciplined and optimal play here.

I am in the Button position with 6d-5s. While these are suited connecters, they are weak and disconnected from any high-card strength. I have already folded in the small blind (Pro) and big blind (Flash) is active, meaning I only need to call 100 more to see a flop. Given the pot size and the relative strength of my hand, this is a standard defensive play in position. I will call to see if I can hit a favorable flop.

I have 5h 3d offsuit in the Small Blind, which is a very weak hand with extremely low equity and playability. Facing a limp from the Button, there is no reason to put more chips into the pot out of position, even if it's just completing for half a big blind. Folding is the only sensible choice.

I am in the big blind with 3h 5d. Flashlight on the button has limped into the pot, and the small blind has folded. Since I am already in the hand and there is no additional cost to see the flop, checking is the standard play with this weak holding.

I have 5-high on a 7-T-J rainbow board. This board texture is very likely to have connected with a limping range, and I have absolutely no equity or draws. In a limped pot out of position, the most prudent move is to check and see what the opponent does. I have no reason to bluff into a wet board.

I am holding 6d-5s on a board of 7d-Td-Jc. I have very little equity here, only a weak backdoor diamond draw. Since Flash (the big blind) checked, I am in position, but I have no reason to bet into a board that is likely to have hit the big blind's checking range. Checking behind is the most prudent play to maintain a cheap realization of my hand's potential, if any, or to fold to future aggression.

I have 5-high on a highly connected board of 7-T-J-Q. My hand has no equity and no draws. Given that Flashlight limped pre-flop and checked the flop, I will continue to check and hope to see a free river, as betting into this texture with zero equity is not profitable.

I am sitting with 6-5 on a 7-T-J-Q board. I have absolutely no showdown value and no meaningful equity (only a gutshot or backdoor possibility that is not really present). My opponent, Flash, has checked twice. Betting with 6-high into a player who might be checking to trap or just has a weak hand is inefficient. Checking behind allows me to maintain a cheap pot and see the river for free, preserving my stack for a better spot.

The board is 7-T-J-Q-J. I have 5-high and zero showdown value. The pot is very small (250). Flashlight has shown passivity by checking every street. However, with a board this connected, it is highly likely Flashlight has at least a pair or some piece of the board. Since it is a limped pot and I am out of position, I will check and concede if there is a bet, or take the free showdown if possible.

The board is 7d-Td-Jc-Qs-Js. My hand is 6d-5s, which has no showdown value whatsoever against any range. Flash checked the river, and there is no benefit in betting a hand that cannot win at showdown and likely will not force a fold from anything but absolute air, which is unlikely to call anyway. Checking behind allows me to conclude the hand with no further risk.
I am dealt 2h 3d, which is one of the worst possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Being the first to act on the Button, folding is the only profitable play.

I am in a blind vs. blind situation after the button folded. I hold Ace-Seven of hearts, which is a very strong hand in this spot. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to either take down the blinds or play a pot with a significant range advantage against the big blind.

Flash raised from the small blind, and I am in the big blind with suited cards (Kc-9c). The pot odds are favorable, and given that I am the chip leader, I have the stack depth to defend my blind. Calling is the standard and most profitable move here to see a flop with a decent suited hand.

On this flop of 8h 2c 2s, I have the best hand very often with Ace high. The board is quite dry, and as the pre-flop aggressor, I have the range advantage. A continuation bet here is standard to maintain initiative and potentially fold out some of Flashlight's overcards or weak suited connectors that might have equity against me. I will bet 250, which is slightly less than half the pot, a sizing that is effective on such static boards.

The flop is extremely dry with 8h-2c-2s. My opponent (Flash) has made a small continuation bet of 250 into a pot of 600, offering good pot odds. While I only have King-high, the board is uncoordinated, and it is highly likely the opponent is testing the waters with a continuation bet or a weak pair. With a significant stack advantage, calling is the most balanced approach to see if the turn provides a more favorable card or if the opponent slows down.

The King of Hearts is a significant scare card that favors my pre-flop raising range. It also provides me with the nut flush draw, giving me excellent semi-bluffing equity. By betting here, I exert maximum pressure on Flashlight's likely holdings such as mid-pairs or an 8, which now find themselves in a difficult spot. If called, I still have numerous outs to the nut flush or potentially an Ace on the river to win the pot.

I have top pair with Kings (Kc in hand, Kh on board). The opponent has been aggressive throughout the tournament. While I could be beat by a better King or a trapped trips (2s or 8s), I have too much equity to fold to a single bet on the turn. Raising would likely only get called by better hands or force folds from hands I am already beating, turning my hand into a bluff. Calling keeps the pot manageable and allows me to see the river, keeping the opponent's bluffing range wide.

On the river, the board is 8h 2c 2s Kh 5d. I have Ace high with Ah 7h. After betting both the flop and the turn and being called by Flashlight both times, it is unlikely that another bet will force him to fold a better hand like an 8, a King, or a pocket pair, especially given his large stack. While I represented a strong hand, his persistence suggests he has at least some showdown value. I will check to see if I can win at showdown against missed draws or to minimize my losses if he has the better hand. If he bets, I will likely fold as Ace high is not a strong bluff-catcher here.

Flash checked the river after being aggressive on the flop and turn. This suggests either a 'give up' on a bluff or that he has a marginal showdown value hand that he is afraid to bet. Since I have top pair (Kings), I have decent showdown value. Betting here might be risky if he is trapping with a 2 or a better King, and it would likely only be called by hands that beat me. Checking back is the safest and most profitable play to take the pot with my pair of Kings, as it avoids a potential check-raise and collects the pot against his missed bluffs.

I have Ace-Ten offsuit on the button in a three-handed game, which is a strong starting hand. I will raise to 300, consistent with my previous opening sizes, to exert pressure on the blinds and build the pot with a hand that likely has significant equity against their defending ranges.

Holding 2s5s in the small blind against a button raise is a clear fold. The hand has very little equity, and playing out of position with such a marginal holding is generally a losing strategy. It is best to fold and wait for a better spot.

AQs is a premium starting hand. Facing a Button open from Flash, I want to 3-bet for value and fold equity out of position. A 3-bet to 1000 puts significant pressure on his button opening range, often winning the pot pre-flop or building a pot with a very strong hand if called.

I have Ace-Ten offsuit on the Button, which is a strong hand in three-handed play. After my initial raise, the Big Blind (Pro) has put in a 3-bet to 1000. Given our stack sizes and the fact that I have position, calling 700 more to see a flop is a standard play. Ace-Ten has good equity against a wide 3-betting range in this short-handed format, and I can leverage my position to make better decisions on the subsequent streets.

On this relatively dry, paired flop in a 3-bet pot, I possess a strong range advantage, as my 3-betting range contains all the big overpairs (AA-TT). A small continuation bet with my entire range, including Ace-Queen high, puts pressure on Flash's floating range and denies equity to hands like King-Jack or suited connectors that missed. Betting around 25% of the pot (500 chips) efficiently folds out his air while keeping my risk low if he decides to raise or shove.

On a paired board of 6-2-6, I have Ace-high with a Ten kicker. The opponent's 3-bet range pre-flop likely includes many high cards (AK, AQ, AJ) and pocket pairs. A bet of 500 into a pot of 2050 is a relatively small continuation bet (roughly 1/4 pot). Given the pot odds (5:1) and my position, calling is the most appropriate move. Folding would be too tight given that many of my opponent's 3-betting bluffs or overcards like KQ or suited connectors have missed this board entirely, and I still have six outs to potentially improve to the best hand if I'm currently behind a smaller pocket pair or another Ace.

The 4s on the turn is largely a brick. After Flash calls my small flop c-bet, his range is weighted towards pocket pairs (77-JJ), flush draws, and perhaps some floats like AK. My Ace-Queen high still has decent showdown value against missed draws and floats, but I lack the equity to profitably barrel his remaining stack of 3550 (an SPR of about 1.1). Checking allows me to pot control, keep his weaker hands and bluffs in the pot, and potentially realize my 6 outs to an overcard if he decides to check back.

On the turn, the board is 6h-2d-6d-4s and the opponent (Pro) has checked after a small continuation bet on the flop. My Ace-Ten high still has significant showdown value against a 3-betting range that includes many whiffed broadway cards (like KQ, KJ, or AJ). By checking back, I control the size of the pot and get to see the river for free, which is beneficial since I am currently ahead of his bluffs and behind his value hands (pocket pairs or a 6). Betting here would likely only get called by better hands and would force out hands that I am already beating.

The river brings a third six to the board, leaving me with trip sixes and an Ace-Queen kicker. Flash's turn check back caps his range, indicating he likely holds a medium pocket pair (77-JJ) for pot control, or missed floats and flush draws. Since any pocket pair makes a full house on this board, betting would mean turning my hand into a bluff and risking my stack to fold out his marginal pairs. By checking, I pot control, easily realize my showdown equity against his missed draws (which I beat), and safely evaluate if he decides to bluff or value bet.

On the river, the board is 6h-2d-6d-4s-6s, giving me three of a kind with sixes and an Ace-Ten kicker (6-6-6-A-T). My opponent (Pro) 3-bet pre-flop and then checked both the turn and the river. While my hand is relatively strong, any pocket pair in the opponent's range (like 77-AA) has now improved to a full house. Additionally, hands like AK, AQ, and AJ also beat me by having a better kicker for the trips. Since there is very little value in betting—I am unlikely to be called by a worse hand or to force a better hand to fold—checking back to see a showdown is the most prudent play. My Ace-high still has value against missed broadway hands like KQ or QJ that Pro might have 3-bet with pre-flop.

In a 3-handed game, 10-9 offsuit on the button is a strong enough hand to open, especially given my substantial chip lead over my opponents. My large stack allows me to play aggressively and put pressure on the blinds. A standard raise to 200 (the minimum raise) is appropriate here to either steal the blinds or play the hand with the initiative in position.

I am in the Small Blind with K2o. The Button (Flashlight) has min-raised to 200. K2o is a weak hand to play out of position, and there's no reason to defend or 3-bet as a bluff here. Folding is the correct choice to preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with 7h 4c, which is a very weak hand. Flashlight opened from the button for a minimum raise of 200. Although I am getting 3.5:1 pot odds to call, my hand has poor playability post-flop and I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand. Given my relatively short stack of 3,450 (34.5 BB), it is better to fold and wait for a stronger hand to commit chips.
AQo is a premium starting hand, especially 3-handed and on the Button. I will open with a standard raise to build the pot and take control of the hand.

I am in the Small Blind with Jc 9s. The Button (Pro) has opened for a standard raise to 250. While J9o is a decent hand in 3-handed play, it performs poorly out of position, especially with a stack of 34BB. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop against Pro, and also leaves me vulnerable to a squeeze from the Big Blind (Flashlight), who has a massive stack. I will fold to preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

The hand 9h-5h is quite weak, even when suited. Calling 150 into a 400-chip pot out of position against an aggressive button is not optimal, especially when holding a significant chip lead. It is better to fold and wait for a more favorable situation.

I am on the button with Ten-Eight offsuit. With a stack of 3400 (34 BB), I am the shortest stack at the table. While Ten-Eight is a marginal opening hand in a three-handed game, the two larger stacks (Flashlight and Pro) have been fairly active and willing to defend or re-raise. Given my current position and the strength of this hand, I prefer to fold and wait for a more robust hand to commit my chips, preserving my stack for better opportunities to double up.

With the Button having folded, I am in the Small Blind against the Big Blind (Pro). Holding Ten-Four suited is a marginal hand, but it is a standard opportunity to steal the blinds. Raising to 300 gives me a good chance to take down the pot pre-flop. If the Big Blind calls, I will evaluate the flop, but I have a positional disadvantage, so I will play cautiously.

Facing a 3x raise from the Small Blind, Q5s is generally too weak to defend profitably. Although we will have position post-flop, the hand lacks connectivity and strong high-card value. It's best to fold and preserve our stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button with Ace-Ten offsuit, which is a strong holding in a three-handed game. Opening with a raise is the optimal strategic play to take control of the hand, utilize my positional advantage, and build the pot. I have a healthy stack and will continue with my aggressive pre-flop strategy.
I am in the Small Blind facing a 3x open raise from the Button. My hand is 8d 4h, which is extremely weak, offsuit, and disconnected. This is an automatic fold in any standard strategy.

I am in the Big Blind with 92o, which is a very weak hand. The button (Flashlight) has raised to 300. Although the pot odds are roughly 2.25 to 1, 92o performs poorly post-flop and I would be playing out of position against a large stack. Conserving my remaining 33 big blinds for a better starting hand is the most prudent strategy here.

I am first to act on the Button 3-handed with A5s. This is a very strong starting hand in a short-handed dynamic, well ahead of my opponents' defending ranges. I will make a standard open-raise to 2.5x (250) to steal the blinds or play a pot in position with a hand that has excellent equity and playability post-flop.

I am in the Small Blind with 7h 4s, which is a very weak starting hand. Pro has opened with a raise from the Button. Given my stack size and the poor quality of my hand, there is no reason to defend or bluff here. Folding is the standard and most profitable play.

I am in the Big Blind with a very weak hand, 4-7 offsuit. While the raise from the button is relatively small, calling out of position with such a disconnected, low-value hand is generally unprofitable. Given the depth of stacks and the poor quality of my hole cards, the most disciplined play is to fold and wait for a more favorable situation.

I am on the button with T5o, which is a very weak starting hand even in a three-handed game. My stack is the shortest at 32.5 BB, and while the button should generally play aggressively, T5o is too weak to justify an open-raise or a call. I will wait for a better spot to commit my chips.

I am in the small blind and the button has folded. With K8 offsuit, I have a reasonable hand to attempt a blind steal. As the chip leader, I should use my position and stack size to apply pressure on the big blind. Raising to 300 is a standard move to take down the 150 chip pot pre-flop or to dictate the action.

I am in the Big Blind with 3d 6h, which is a very weak and unplayable hand. The Small Blind raised to 300. Even though I would have position post-flop, my hand has virtually no equity or playability to justify calling a raise. The best decision is to fold and wait for a better spot.

Being on the button with suited connectors, I have a clear opportunity to steal the blinds, especially given my chip lead and the short stack of the big blind. A raise to 250 is a standard size to put pressure on my opponents while maintaining a balanced approach to my button opening range.

K5s in the small blind facing a raise from the button is too weak to call out of position. 3-bet bluffing is unnecessarily risky against the chip leader, especially with the big blind sitting on a shorter stack who might squeeze or shove over the top. Folding is the standard and correct play.

I have T9o in the Big Blind. Flashlight, the chip leader on the button, has raised to 250. Given that I only need to call 150 more into a pot of 400, I am getting roughly 2.67-to-1 odds. T9o is a strong enough hand to defend my big blind against a standard button opening range. I will call to see a flop.

I have Ten-high with no draws on a Queen-high flop. My opponent, Flashlight, was the pre-flop aggressor from the button. I have no reason to lead into them with this hand, as I have almost zero equity. I will check and most likely fold if they make a bet, as I need to preserve my 30bb stack for better opportunities.

I am in position on a relatively dry flop. With 7d-3d, I have little equity, but this board (Q-5-4) is unlikely to have connected strongly with the big blind's calling range, making it a good candidate for a continuation bet to take down the pot immediately. A bet of 250 into a 550 pot represents a standard C-bet sizing to force a fold from my opponent.

I have Ten-high with no pair and no significant draws on a Queen-high board. Flashlight, the chip leader and pre-flop aggressor, has made a standard continuation bet. I have very little equity in this pot and no reason to continue. I will fold and preserve my 30bb stack for a better opportunity.

I am on the Button with ATo in a 3-handed game. This is a clear open-raise. A standard sizing of 2.5x (250 chips) works well here to steal the blinds or play a pot in position with a strong broadway hand.

I am in the Small Blind with a pocket pair of fours (4c 4d). The Button (Pro) has raised to 2.5x the big blind. With a stack of roughly 29.5 BBs, I have sufficient implied odds to set-mine, although being out of position makes this slightly less ideal. Given the tendencies of 'Pro' and the massive stack of 'Flashlight' in the Big Blind, calling is a standard play here to see a flop. If I hit a set, I can potentially double through Pro. If the Big Blind squeezes, I'll have to evaluate based on the size, but for now, calling 200 more into a pot of 400 is mathematically sound for set-mining purposes.
The pot is offering 4:1 odds, making it a mathematically sound call to see a flop with 6-10 offsuit. I am already in for the big blind, so closing the action with these odds is standard.

On a flop of 9h-5h-Ts, my pocket fours are now an underpair to three cards. I am out of position against two opponents. This board is quite dynamic with flush and straight draws, and it is highly likely that at least one opponent has connected with a pair of Tens, Nines, or a high draw. Betting here as a bluff is risky into two players, and my hand has very little equity to continue if raised. The standard and most prudent play is to check and evaluate the action behind me. If there is a significant bet, I will likely fold, as 44 is very vulnerable on this texture.

I have top pair with a weak kicker on a board with a flush draw present. Since I am in the Big Blind in a multi-way pot, checking is the most prudent strategy to see how the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) acts. I want to keep the pot controlled given the marginal nature of my hand strength.

With top pair, top kicker on a wet board (flush and straight draws available), I need to bet for value and protection after both opponents checked. A bet of around half the pot will charge the draws and get value from worse pairs like a worse Ten or a Nine.

On this 9h-5h-Ts flop, my pocket fours are an underpair to the entire board. The pre-flop raiser (Pro) has followed up with a continuation bet into two players. This board texture heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range, which includes many Tens, Nines, and overpairs, as well as numerous strong draws (flushes and straights). My hand has very little equity to improve (only two outs for a set) and I am out of position. Calling 350 chips when I have a limited stack is not a profitable long-term play given the likelihood that I am already beaten and the fact that the Big Blind still has to act behind me. Folding is the most disciplined and correct move to preserve my remaining stack for better opportunities.

I have middle pair (tens) on a board that contains flush draws and potential straight draws. The pot is 850 and the bet is 350, offering me about 3.4:1 pot odds. My hand is strong enough to justify a call, but not strong enough to raise, especially against a pre-flop aggressor. Calling allows me to realize my equity and re-evaluate on the turn.

I have a pair of Tens with a weak kicker on a board that just brought an Ace on the turn. This Ace is a very likely card for the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) to have connected with or to be representing. My pair of Tens is significantly weakened by this card, and betting would be a mistake as it only isolates me against better hands. Checking is the most prudent action to control the pot size and to see how my opponent reacts to the changing board texture.

The turn brings the Ace of Hearts, giving me top two pair (Aces and Tens) but also completing the flush draw on a very wet board (9h 5h Ts Ah). Since I don't hold a heart, my hand is vulnerable to made flushes, and I have minimal redraws to improve if I am behind. While I can get value from worse two pairs or pair-plus-draw hands, betting risks getting check-raised by a flush or a set, which would put me in a terrible spot. Checking here allows for pot control, keeps my opponent's bluffs or weaker hands in their range, and lets me evaluate the river safely.

The board is extremely wet with four hearts, meaning any player holding a single heart has a flush. My pair of Tens is a marginal hand that is very likely beaten. Betting here would likely only be called by hands that beat me, so checking to realize a free showdown is the most prudent strategy.

The river brings a fourth heart to the board. Holding Ac Td, my two pair (Aces and Tens) is extremely vulnerable, as any single heart in my opponent's hand makes a flush. Since I do not hold a heart, betting for value is out of the question, as better hands will call or raise, and worse hands will fold. Checking behind is the safest and most standard play to realize whatever showdown value my hand has left.

I am on the button with 9-4 offsuit. This is a weak starting hand. While it is only 100 more to call into a pot of 150, I have a very short stack (2750 chips) compared to the blinds (17425 and 9675). With a stack-to-blind ratio of 27.5 BBs, I need to be more selective with the hands I play to avoid bleeding chips. Folding is the most prudent play here to preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

I am in the small blind with Ah8h, a strong hand for head-up play against the big blind. The button has already folded, so I only need to contend with Pro. Given my significant chip lead and the positional advantage post-flop, raising is the best strategy to capitalize on the situation, build the pot, or take it down immediately.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Seven offsuit, which is a very weak and disconnected starting hand. Facing a raise from the Small Blind (who is the chip leader and applying pressure), defending with this hand is unprofitable because it plays poorly post-flop and is often dominated. Folding is the correct decision here.

I am on the button with King-Seven offsuit. This hand has low equity and is generally considered weak to play aggressively in a 3-handed game. Given I have a substantial stack, there is no need to force action with a marginal holding. I will fold to preserve my stack.

AQ suited is a premium starting hand. Being in the small blind after the button folds, I want to raise for value and to build the pot. A raise to 300 is standard to put pressure on the big blind, and given their short stack of around 2650 chips, I am more than happy to call if they decide to shove.

I have been dealt pocket treys (3s 3d) in the Big Blind. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300, and the Button has folded. I am currently the short stack with 26.5 big blinds. Calling here is a solid play as I will have position on Pro for the rest of the hand, and I'm getting correct pot odds to see a flop. While 33 is vulnerable to overcards, its value increases in 3-handed play, and I can evaluate the strength of my hand after the flop based on Pro's betting patterns and the board texture. I will call the additional 200 chips to close the pre-flop action.

The flop comes King-high and is very dry, which heavily favors my pre-flop raising range. A small continuation bet of 1/3 pot should be enough to fold out his missed overcards and random floats, while keeping my risk low if he happens to hold a King or a pocket pair. This allows me to take down the pot frequently without committing too many chips.

I have pocket treys on a relatively dry board of 2h-Ks-6d. Pro has made a small continuation bet of 200 into a 600-chip pot (1/3 pot sizing). This sizing is common for a wide range of hands on such a texture. While I am behind any King or Six, I am ahead of most of Pro's missed overcards like AQ, AJ, or suited connectors. Given that I have position on Pro for the rest of the hand and I am receiving 5:1 pot odds to call, folding would be too tight. I will call to see how the turn develops and potentially reach a cheap showdown or improve to a set.

The turn brings the Jack of diamonds, giving me a massive combo draw with the nut flush draw and a gutshot straight draw, along with two overcards. I have immense equity and fold equity. Firing a second barrel here applies heavy pressure on Flash's marginal made hands (like a weak King, a Jack, or a middle pair) and builds the pot for when I hit. With Flash's stack at around 2250 and the pot at 1000, a half-pot bet of 500 perfectly sets up a potential river shove.

The turn Jack is a very unfavorable card for my pocket treys, adding another overcard to the board. Pro's second barrel of 500 (half the pot) suggests either a strong hand (Kx, Jx) or a semi-bluff with significant equity. My 33 is now an underpair to three cards on the board and is unlikely to be ahead. Considering I am the short stack with about 22 big blinds remaining, calling here would commit a significant portion of my stack with very little chance of winning the hand. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my tournament life for a better opportunity.
I am dealt a very weak offsuit gapper (75o) on the Button. This hand has very little playability and equity, so the standard and optimal play is to fold pre-flop.

I am in the Small Blind with 4c 6s, a very weak offsuit hand. The Button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind. With a stack of 2200 (22 BB), I need to be selective about the hands I play. Even getting 3:1 pot odds to call the remaining 50 chips, 46o performs poorly post-flop and is likely dominated by the Big Blind's range. I will fold to preserve my stack for a better starting hand.

With a stack of 2200 (22 BB), Q3o is far too weak to open from the button. While I need to find opportunities to build my stack, this hand has poor playability and very little showdown value if called. I will fold and wait for a stronger range to commit chips.
I am in the Small Blind and it has been folded to me. My hand (2c 8h) is weak, but completing the small blind is a standard, low-risk move to see a flop cheaply when heads-up against the Big Blind.

Flashlight just limped from the Small Blind, giving me the option to check and see a free flop from the Big Blind. My 75o is a very weak hand, so there's no reason to build a pot or attempt a marginal isolation raise. Checking is the safest and most standard choice.

I am holding 8-high with no pair on an Ace-high board. While I have a backdoor flush draw, it is extremely weak. This board texture heavily favors the Big Blind, as they likely have many Ace-high hands or pairs in their range. Since I am out of position and have very little equity, checking is the standard play to minimize losses. I will look to fold if there is any aggression from the opponent.

I have absolutely nothing on this Ace-high board, no pair and no meaningful draw. With Flashlight checking to me, checking behind is the safest and most standard play to control the pot size and see a free turn card.

I am in the Small Blind with 2c 8h on a board of Ah 3h 9c Ks. The pot is currently 200, and I have a weak flush draw. With an Ace and a King on the board, my opponent's range is heavily protected, making a bluff unsuccessful and risky. Checking allows me to realize my equity for free if the opponent also checks, or to evaluate the river. I will check to minimize risk.

The turn card is a King, adding another high card to the board. I still have absolutely nothing with 7-high and no draws. Since Flashlight checked again, there is no reason to put money into the pot on a bluff. Checking behind to see a free river is the best play.

The hand has gone to the river with no betting on any street. I have 8-high with a pair of 3s on the board, which is extremely weak. Since my opponent checked, the only rational play is to check behind to go to showdown. There is no value in betting as I will only be called by hands that beat me, and I cannot expect a fold from any hand that might have equity.

I have 7-high on a board with an Ace and a King. The pot is very small since we both checked down the flop and turn. Although my hand has zero showdown value, attempting to bluff at this point is unnecessary and likely slightly -EV, as Flashlight might stubbornly call with a weak pair or a better high card. Checking behind and conceding this small pot is the most standard and risk-free option.

I am holding 3h 6s, which is a very weak hand with no playability. As the button, I have the luxury of waiting for better spots. There is no incentive to call or raise with such garbage cards, so I will fold to preserve my stack.

AQo is a premium starting hand, especially blind vs. blind. With the Button folding, I have a clear opportunity to raise for value and take the initiative against the Big Blind. Raising to 300 (3 big blinds) is a standard sizing that builds the pot while allowing Flash to continue with worse hands.

I am in the Big Blind with 7d 3s, which is a very weak hand. Pro, who is the small blind, has raised to 300. Given my current stack size of 2100 chips and the poor quality of my hand, there is no reason to defend this blind or attempt a bluff against a player with a significant chip lead. I will fold to preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

I am dealt 2s 6c on the Button. This is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em (trash offsuit). It has virtually no playability or equity. The optimal play is to fold immediately and preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with KJo, which is a very strong hand in a blind-versus-blind situation after the Button has folded. With a stack of approximately 20 big blinds, I need to play aggressively to build my stack or take down the blinds. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the Big Blind.

I am in the Big Blind with 5-4 offsuit, which is a very weak, uncoordinated hand. Although I have a large stack relative to the small blind, there is no strategic advantage to calling a raise from the small blind with such a poor hand out of position. Folding is the most disciplined and prudent play to preserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

I am on the button with 8h 5d and a stack of 2200 chips (22 big blinds). While this hand is weak, the table has been playing relatively conservatively pre-flop. However, given my short stack compared to Flashlight and Pro, I should be selective with my opening ranges to avoid bleeding chips. 85o is not a strong enough hand to raise from the button in a 3-handed game with these stack depths, as I'm likely to face a 3-bet or be forced to fold to post-flop aggression.

With J-10 offsuit in the small blind after the button folds, I am in a favorable heads-up position against the big blind. Raising to 300 is a standard play to apply pressure, potentially win the blinds immediately, or take the initiative into the flop with a solid, playable hand.

T7o is a weak, disconnected, and unsuited hand. Facing a 3x raise from the Small Blind, defending with such a marginal hand will be unprofitable in the long run due to its poor equity and lack of post-flop playability.

I am on the button with Queen-Ten suited, which is a strong hand for this position. Given the stack depths and the fact that I am in position, a standard raise to 300 is appropriate to try and take down the blinds or play a pot with the initiative.

I have Queen-Deuce offsuit in the Small Blind. This is a very weak hand with low equity. Facing a 3BB raise from the Button (Flashlight), the correct play is to simply fold and save my chips. Q2o plays terribly post-flop and doesn't warrant defending.

I am currently the short stack with 2100 chips (21 big blinds). Flashlight, the chip leader, has raised to 300 from the button. Ace-Eight offsuit (A8o) is a very strong hand in a three-handed game, especially for a short stack defending the big blind. Calling would leave me out of position with a shallow stack-to-pot ratio, which is difficult to play optimally. By shoving all-in (2100 total), I maximize my fold equity and ensure I realize the full equity of my hand. Given Flashlight's likely wide opening range from the button, A8o is likely ahead of their range.

I opened to 300 with suited broadway cards (QhTh) on the button. Flash (the Big Blind) responded with a significant 3-bet to 2100. Given my position and the strength of my hand, folding is too tight, and 4-betting is unnecessarily risky. Calling the raise allows me to see a flop with a playable hand, maintain my position advantage, and control the pot size while evaluating post-flop connectivity.

I have Top Pair (Aces) with an 8-high Flush Draw on a board containing three clubs. Having committed almost my entire stack pre-flop, I only have 100 chips remaining in a pot of 4,250. This is a mandatory commitment of my final chips regardless of the board texture, but hitting Top Pair and a flush draw makes the decision even more straightforward. I will bet my remaining 100 chips.

The opponent has made a min-bet of 100 into a pot of 4500. This gives me pot odds of 46:1 to call. Even though I have no pair or active draw on this flop with QhTh, the price is so cheap that folding is mathematically incorrect, and raising is unnecessary as it bloats the pot with a weak hand when I can simply call and evaluate the turn. I will call to maintain my position and see if I can improve or find a better spot to maneuver.
Jd 6d is a very weak starting hand, even from the button. It plays poorly post-flop and doesn't have enough equity to justify an open raise. Folding is the safest and most standard play.

With the Button (Pro) folding, the action is on me in the Small Blind. I am holding Ten of Diamonds and Trey of Diamonds (Td 3d). While this is a suited hand, it is very weak (T-high with a poor kicker) and I would be playing the rest of the hand out of position against the chip leader, Flashlight. Given my current stack of 44 big blinds, there is no need to take unnecessary risks with such a marginal hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.
I am on the button with 9s 3d, which is an extremely weak hand. With a stack of 4400 (44 BB), there is no reason to play this hand into two larger stacks. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

With the button having folded, I am in the small blind against the big blind. With Jh4h, I have a suited hand that is worth completing to see a cheap flop in a heads-up pot. The pot is currently 150, and it costs me 50 more to call, which is a standard play to defend the small blind.

I am in the Big Blind with 72o, one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. The Small Blind has just limped in. I have the option to check and see a free flop without committing any additional chips. There is absolutely no reason to raise with this holding.

I am in the small blind holding bottom pair (pair of 4s) on an Ace-high board with two diamonds. My opponent in the big blind checked to me. With a weak pair and no kicker advantage, betting into an Ace-high board is likely to get called by an Ace or a better pair, while forcing out hands I currently beat. Checking allows me to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the turn.

I have bottom pair on a monotone diamond board with no diamond in my hand. Betting here has very little value as I will typically only get called by better hands or strong draws. Checking behind is the safest and most standard play to keep the pot small.

I am in the small blind with a pair of 4s on a board showing an Ace and three diamonds. My hand is extremely weak in this context, and any bet I make will likely only be called by hands that beat me (any Ace, any pair, or any hand with a diamond). Checking is the standard play here to keep the pot small, minimize losses, and hopefully reach a cheap showdown.

I hit two pair on the turn with Sevens and Deuces. Although the board is a monotone diamond flop and I hold no diamonds, Flashlight has checked both the flop and the turn, indicating he likely doesn't have a strong made flush or a powerful Ace. Betting here allows me to extract value from single diamond draws, weaker pairs, or straight draws, while also protecting my hand against free river cards that could easily counterfeit or beat my two pair. I will bet half the pot to test the waters.

The opponent (Pro) is betting 100 into a pot of 300 on the turn after checking the flop. This small sizing suggests a probe bet or a weak made hand rather than a monster, and my pair of 4s has decent showdown value in this spot. Given the pot odds, folding is too conservative, and raising is unnecessary as it would likely isolate me against hands that beat me. Calling is the best way to control the pot and evaluate on the river.

I am first to act on the river with a pair of 4s on an Ace-high, relatively coordinated board. My hand has very little showdown value, as almost any Ace, 10, or 7 beats me. Betting would be value-cutting myself, as I would only get called by better hands. Checking allows me to reach a showdown as cheaply as possible or fold if the opponent bets, minimizing further losses.

I have bottom two pair (Sevens and Deuces) on a runout that did not complete the flush draw. I am in the Big Blind with a very weak offsuit hand (2h9s). The Small Blind limped. I have the option to check and see a free flop. There is no reason to raise and bloat the pot with this trash hand, so checking is the obvious play.

I have completely missed a very coordinated, high-card board (Ac Kd Qd) with my 9-high. I have completely missed this board, which now features four to a straight and three diamonds. I have 9-high on a board with two Kings, an Ace, and a Queen, plus three diamonds. My hand has absolutely no showdown value. King of Spades and Four of Clubs is a weak, unsuited hand that does not warrant an investment of 100 chips from the button. Given the current stack size and the desire to maximize returns, folding is the most disciplined and profitable play.

I am in the Small Blind with 92o, one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. With Flashlight folding on the button, there's no reason to invest any chips to play this trash hand out of position against the Big Blind, even for a steal. Folding is the standard and correct play.

J7 offsuit is a weak starting hand. Even with the advantage of position on the Button, it is not strong enough to profitably open or call against the blinds. Folding to preserve my stack for better spots is the optimal play.

I am in the Big Blind facing a raise from the Small Blind (Flash). The pot is 400, and I need to call 200 to see the flop. I have top pair with a 9 kicker on a board of Tc-Qd-2d. This is a relatively favorable flop for my hand, although there are several draws available (flush and straight). I am in the Big Blind holding bottom pair on a board of Tc Qd 2d. Flash (Small Blind) bet 300 into a 600 pot, giving me 3:1 pot odds to call. The Ten of Spades on the turn pairs the middle card of the flop. While I still have top pair (Queens), the board now presents the possibility of trips for any Ten in my opponent's range. I am in the Big Blind with a pair of deuces on a board of Tc-Qd-2d-Ts. The river card is the 5 of clubs, which is a complete brick and does not change the strength of my hand. I am in the Big Blind with a pair of deuces on a paired board of Tc-Qd-2d-Ts-5c. Flash checked the river, signaling weakness. I have been dealt pocket Kings, the second-strongest starting hand. My initial raise was met with a 3-bet to 900 from Flashlight. I have top pair with the nut flush draw in a 4-bet pot. My opponent is extremely short-stacked, with only 850 chips remaining in a 4300 pot. With the turn being the Nine of Spades, the board is now Jack of Clubs, Five of Diamonds, Ace of Spades, Nine of Spades. I have Top Pair (Ace) and a nut flush draw (Ace of Clubs). I have pocket Kings, but the flop brought an Ace, which is a significant part of my opponent's 4-bet calling range. My pair of Aces with a Ten kicker is a marginal hand that has some showdown value, but it is not strong enough to value bet against a 4-bet pre-flop range. Since there is almost no value in betting—as I would only be called by better hands and fold out worse—checking back to realize my equity and reach showdown is the most prudent play.

I am holding King-Four offsuit, which is a weak hand in 3-handed play. Even from the button, there is no strategic incentive to play this holding against the blinds. Folding conserves my chip lead and avoids engaging in a potentially negative expected value pot with a marginal starting hand.

As the Small Blind with the Button having folded, I only need to call 50 more to match the Big Blind's 100. Given my large stack size and the pot odds, calling with 8c6h is a reasonable and low-risk way to see a flop in this blind-vs-blind situation.

With 9sTh in the Big Blind against a Small Blind limp, I have a decently connected but marginal hand. Checking gives me a free look at the flop in position, which is a very standard and low-risk play.

I am in the Small Blind position with 8-high on a board of 3s, 3c, 2c. I have no pair and no draw. I am in the Small Blind with 8-high on a board of 3s, 3c, 2c, 9h. The flop was checked through, and there is no reason to bet now as I have no showdown value and no draw. Checking is the standard play to keep the pot small, minimize risk, and potentially see the river for free.

The turn gives me top pair with my 9 on a 3-3-2-9 board. Flashlight has checked twice, showing significant weakness. I have 8-high with no pair and no meaningful draw on a board of 3s, 3c, 2c, 9h. The opponent checked the flop behind me and is now betting on the turn, which is likely a probe bet after the 9h. Since I have no showdown value and no equity to improve, calling is a waste of chips. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I am on the button with a Queen-high hand. Given my position and significant chip lead, raising to 300 is a standard opening move to attempt to steal the blinds and take control of the hand before the flop.
I am dealt Jack-Deuce suited in the Small Blind. Flashlight has opened for 3x the big blind from the Button. J2s is highly disconnected and plays very poorly post-flop, making it an easy fold.

With 75s on the button, I have a marginal suited one-gapper. I am in the Small Blind with 10h 7h, a suited hand that plays reasonably well in heads-up situations. My hand, 8d-2d, is extremely weak, even in the Big Blind. While the price to call (200 chips) is decent, being out of position against an opponent with a short stack makes playing this hand unfavorable. It is better to conserve my stack and wait for a more playable starting hand.

I am on the button with Ace-Six offsuit. With 20 big blinds remaining and only 15 hands left in the tournament, I need to be aggressive to build my stack. Ace-Six is a standard opening hand from the button in a three-handed game. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to pick up the blinds or play the hand in position.

J6s is a weak hand, especially out of position in a multiway pot against a short-stacked pre-flop raiser and a big stack who already called. Calling here is negative EV, so folding is the standard and correct play.

The Button's range is wide, but he is representing strength by betting into two players (even though one folded), and continuing without equity is bleeding chips. The disciplined play is to fold and conserve my stack for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with 9-7 offsuit, which is a weak hand. However, given my dominant stack and the position, a standard opening raise is a common strategic move to attempt to steal the blinds and maintain aggression. I will raise to 300 to test my opponents.

A3 offsuit is a weak holding, especially when playing out of position in the Small Blind. Facing a 3x open from the Button and with a short-stacked Big Blind who could easily squeeze or shove, folding is the most solid and standard decision. Calling would leave me out of position with a dominated Ace, and 3-betting is too risky given the BB's stack size.

I am currently the short stack with 2,300 chips (23 BB) as we approach the end of the tournament (hand 137 of 150). Flashlight has raised from the button to 300. Calling allows me to see a flop and potentially check-shove if I hit top pair or a strong draw, while preserving some stack if the flop is completely unfavorable.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Six offsuit on a board of King-Ten-Seven with two spades. This board texture is very favorable for the pre-flop aggressor (Flashlight on the button) and does not connect with my hand at all. I have no pair and no real draw. I will check and plan to fold to any significant bet, as I have almost no equity here.

I have bottom pair (7s) on a K-T-7 board, which is a vulnerable hand. Checking back is the most prudent action as it allows me to control the pot size and see the turn card for free while avoiding bloating a pot where my hand's relative strength is low.

On the turn, the 6s gives me bottom pair (sixes). However, the 6s also completes a potential flush for any spade in the opponent's range. Flashlight checked back on the flop, which suggests they might not have a very strong hand like a King or a high flush draw, but they could still have a Ten or a medium spade. With only 2,100 chips remaining and a highly coordinated board, I will check to see if I can reach a cheap showdown or get a free river. Leading out into a board with three spades and multiple straight draws is risky with only bottom pair.

I am holding a pair of sevens on a board of seven of spades, ten of spades, king of diamonds, and six of spades. With three spades on the board, my hand is highly vulnerable to any flush, straight, or better pair. Since my opponent has checked to me again, checking behind is the most disciplined play to control the pot size and reach a showdown without committing unnecessary chips to a potentially losing hand.

I have improved to Trip Sixes on the river. Flashlight checked back on both the flop and the turn after raising pre-flop, which indicates a weak range—likely missing the King or the flush. I am now ahead of almost all of Flashlight's checking range (Ten-x, low pocket pairs, Ace-high). I will lead out with a value bet of 300 to extract chips from these marginal hands. It is unlikely they have a flush or a full house given the lack of aggression on previous streets.

My opponent, Flash, checked both the flop and the turn before betting 300 on the river. This line is highly indicative of a probe bet or a bluff, as a strong hand like a King or a Ten would likely have bet earlier for value. While my pair of sevens is vulnerable, it is a decent bluff-catcher given the passive line my opponent took until the river. The pot odds (300 to call a 900 pot) are favorable enough to justify a call to keep my opponent honest, especially given the small bet size in relation to our stack depths.
84o is a very weak starting hand, especially given the stack sizes and position. Folding is the only optimal decision here to preserve my stack.

With the button folding, I am in a blind-versus-blind situation. Jack-Nine offsuit is well above the average strength of hands in this scenario. While I am out of position post-flop, raising allows me to take control of the hand and potentially win the pot immediately without seeing a flop. I will raise to 300, which is a standard opening size to put pressure on the big blind.

Flash has been active from the small blind, and holding Ace-high with a wheel card, I have a strong enough hand to defend my big blind. Given that I have position post-flop and Flash's aggressive style, calling is the most balanced approach to see the flop without inflating the pot unnecessarily with A-5 offsuit.

I have flopped top pair with a Jack kicker on a board that contains some potential draws (diamonds and straight possibilities like 5-6-7 or 6-7-8). Given I am the short stack and currently first to act, I want to bet for value and to protect my hand from Flashlight's potential draws or overcards. A continuation bet of 300, which is half the pot, is a standard and effective sizing here to maintain initiative and gather information about my opponent's hand strength.

The pot is 900 and I have to call 300. I am holding Ace-high with an Ace of Spades, giving me a backdoor flush draw on a board with two diamonds. The board 9d-4d-8c is reasonably connected, but a 1/3 pot c-bet from Flash could be a continuation bluff or a marginal hand. With 1:4 pot odds, calling is a mathematically sound decision to see the turn and reassess, as folding would be too passive given the relatively small bet size.

The Ten of Spades on the turn is an overcard to my pair of Nines and potentially completes some straight draws or improves Flashlight's range (like hands with a Ten). Since I am out of position and my hand strength has relatively decreased, checking is the most cautious and flexible play. This allows me to see how Flashlight responds and potentially get to a cheaper showdown or catch a bluff if he decides to bet.

Flash checked the turn, showing signs of weakness or attempting to pot control. My hand, Ace-high, has very little showdown value against a range that includes pairs like 9s, 8s, 4s, or 10s, all of which are plausible given the board texture. There is no incentive to bet as a bluff because I am not likely to force a fold from a better hand, and I have minimal equity if called. Checking behind allows me to see the river for free and re-evaluate if I improve or if it remains a check-down.

The river Queen is another overcard that significantly weakens my pair of Nines. The board now contains a Queen, a Ten, and an Eight, all of which could have easily hit Flashlight's calling range from the flop. Given that I checked the turn and he checked back, he might have a mediocre hand or a draw that didn't get there, but a bet from me now is unlikely to be called by anything I beat. Conversely, if I bet, I'm rarely getting a better hand to fold. Checking is the most prudent move to try and reach a cheap showdown or potentially fold if he makes a large bet, as my hand strength has plummeted relative to the board texture.

Flash checked the turn and the river, which indicates weakness. With Ace-high, I have some showdown value against any missed draws or complete bluffs in Flash's range. Betting as a bluff is unlikely to succeed, as any pair will call, and checking allows me to realize my equity without risk.

I am on the button with 7-4 offsuit, which is one of the weakest hands in poker.
