Folding is the standard and most disciplined play here to preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

I am in the small blind with Jack-Four offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Even in a heads-up situation against the big blind, playing such a weak hand out of position is not profitable in the long run. Given my substantial chip lead in the tournament, I should focus on playing stronger starting hands and avoiding unnecessary risk.

I am in the button position with 2h5s, which is a very weak hand with no playability. Given my significant stack lead and the poor quality of these hole cards, folding is the most disciplined and profitable decision to preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

With Ten-Deuce offsuit, I have one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Despite the action folding to me in the Small Blind, this hand is simply too poor to play, even for a steal attempt or a limp. Folding is the only optimal and sensible choice here.
With Ace-Jack suited on the Button, I have a very strong starting hand. Raising to 250 is a standard open to build the pot and thin the field, while keeping the sizing reasonable.

I am in the Small Blind with 4h 3s, a very weak hand. The Button (Pro) has opened for a standard 2.5x raise. With the tournament nearing its end (Hand 141 of 150) and being the short stack, I need to be selective about the pots I enter. 43 offsuit has poor equity and will be very difficult to play out of position post-flop. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack for a better doubling-up opportunity.

Pro (Button) has opened to 250. With 62s in the Big Blind, I am getting a reasonable price (150 to call into a total pot of 400) to defend. Given my large stack size and the suited nature of the cards, I will call to see a flop, even though the hand is relatively weak.

The flop is [4d Ac Kd], which is an extremely dry board that strongly favors the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) who likely holds an Ace or a King in their range. My hand, [6s 2s], has no value, no pair, and only minimal backdoor potential. Checking is the only prudent action as I have no reason to lead into the aggressor, and I must proceed with extreme caution if they choose to bet.

With top pair and a strong kicker (AJ) on an A-K-4 board with two diamonds, I have a strong made hand that benefits from betting. A continuation bet of about half the pot will charge draws like diamond flush draws or straight draws (Q-J, Q-T), and can also extract value from a weaker Ace or a King.

I have absolutely no pair, no draw, and no backdoor equity on an Ace-King-high board. Continuing with 6-high on a board that heavily hits the pre-flop aggressor's range would be a massive mistake. Folding is the only logical choice.

I am on the button with a pocket pair (3s 3c). In a 3-handed game, any pocket pair is a strong candidate for an opening raise. I have approximately 36.5 big blinds, which allows for standard positional play. I will raise to 300 to take initiative and potentially win the blinds or see a flop in position.

QTo is a playable hand in the small blind against a button raise. Given my stack size as the chip leader, I can comfortably call to see a flop without needing to commit aggressively pre-flop. This keeps the pot size manageable while retaining the ability to outplay my opponent post-flop.

J8o is a weak, offsuit hand with a gap that plays very poorly out of position, especially against two opponents who have already shown strength (a raise and a call). Even with attractive pot odds, folding is the mathematically correct and standard play here to preserve my stack.

I am out of position on the flop with Q-high and no significant draw against an aggressive opponent who opened from the button. Checking is the most standard and defensive action as I have very little equity to continue if my opponent makes a continuation bet.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and have position. The flop of K-6-5 is a favorable texture for a continuation bet, as it hits my perceived range better than a standard Small Blind flat-calling range. By betting 300 into the 700 pot, I can likely fold out hands like Ace-high or small pairs that missed the board. If I am called, I still have some showdown value with my pocket threes but will likely look to check down unless the board develops favorably.

I am holding Queen-high (Qc, Ts) on a flop of 5c, 6s, Ks. I have no pair and no significant draw, other than a very weak backdoor flush potential. My opponent, the short-stacked button, made a continuation bet into me after I checked. Given my position and the lack of strength in my hand, calling is not a profitable move. As the chip leader, there is no reason to risk chips chasing a weak hand in a bad position against an aggressive player. Folding is the disciplined, optimal play.

I am in the button position with 7-4 offsuit, which is a very weak hand with almost no potential to make a strong range. Given the stage of the tournament (Hand 143/150) and my significant chip stack, there is no reason to waste chips playing this marginal hand. Folding is the disciplined and correct decision.

KQo is a premium hand, especially in a blind versus blind situation. With the button folding, I have a clear opportunity to raise and put pressure on the Big Blind, building the pot with a strong starting hand.

I am in the Big Blind holding Jack-Ten offsuit. The player in the Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. Furthermore, as the Big Blind, I will have the advantage of position for the remainder of the hand. The pot odds are favorable (calling 200 to win a total pot of 600), and my hand has excellent playability. I will call to see the flop and utilize my positional advantage.

I have the second nut flush draw with the King of Spades and two overcards on a monotone board. A small continuation bet puts pressure on the Big Blind, often winning the pot immediately, while keeping the pot manageable in case my opponent already flopped a flush or holds the Ace of Spades. A smaller sizing is standard and effective on monotone boards.

I have top pair with a Jack kicker on a monotone flop of 2s-Ts-8s. Crucially, I also hold the Jack of Spades, giving me the second-nut flush draw. The opponent (Pro) has led for a small sizing (200 into 600), which is a common continuation bet on this texture. Given my top pair and strong flush draw, my hand is too strong to fold. Raising here might isolate me against better hands like sets or made flushes, whereas calling allows me to keep Pro's bluffs in and see how the turn develops with the safety of a draw. I will call the 200.

The turn 6h is a relative blank that doesn't significantly change the board texture. While I have a strong draw to the second nut flush with the King of Spades, I currently only have King-high. Checking out of position controls the pot size and allows me to realize my equity, potentially getting a free river card if the Big Blind checks behind. If facing a bet, the Ks gives me plenty of equity to continue.

I have top pair (tens) and the second-nut flush draw (Jack of Spades) on a very wet board. The opponent, Pro, led on the flop but has now checked on the turn. While my hand is strong, the board texture is dangerous with three spades already present. By checking back, I control the size of the pot and guarantee myself a free look at the river. If a spade or a non-threatening card comes, I can evaluate whether to value bet or call a lead from the opponent. Checking back also protects my range and prevents me from being check-raised off a hand that still has significant equity.

The river Qd gives me top pair with a strong King kicker, and holding the Ks limits the number of flush combinations my opponent can have. Given the check-check on the turn, my opponent's range is primarily composed of marginal made hands like second or third pair (Tx, 8x) or a weak one-spade holding that missed. Since those hands will likely check behind if I check, a medium-sized value bet is the best way to extract value from lighter pairs.

On the river, the board is 2s-Ts-8s-6h-Qd and the pot is 1000 chips. Pro has bet 500, which is exactly half the pot. I have a pair of Tens with a Jack kicker (Js), and importantly, I hold the Jack of Spades. Holding the Jack of Spades is a significant blocker because it reduces the number of flush combinations Pro can have. Pro's line—betting the flop, checking the turn, and then betting the river—often represents a marginal hand that either improved on the river (like a Queen) or is attempting to steal the pot after the turn went check-check. Given that I am getting 3:1 odds on a call (calling 500 to win 2000 total), I only need to be right 25% of the time. In a blind-vs-blind scenario, my pair of Tens is a strong enough bluff-catcher against a wide Small Blind range, especially since the turn check makes a monster hand like a flush less likely for Pro. I will call to see the showdown.
A7s is a strong hand to open from the Button. A standard 2.5x raise allows me to steal the blinds or play a post-flop pot in position with a very playable suited Ace.

In a three-handed game, Ace-Seven offsuit is a strong hand, but being in the Small Blind puts me out of position for the rest of the hand. Pro is an active player on the Button, and his opening range is likely wide. With 30 big blinds, I have the option to 3-bet, but calling allows me to see a flop and control the pot size while the tournament is nearing its conclusion (hand 144 of 150). I am getting good pot odds to call 200 more into a 400 pot.

I am in the big blind with King-Ten offsuit. With the button raising to 250 and the small blind calling, I am getting excellent pot odds (150 to call into a pot of 750). King-Ten is a strong enough hand to defend the big blind here against these ranges. Calling is the standard and most profitable play.

I missed the flop completely. On a paired board of 6-9-6, it's standard to check to the pre-flop aggressor, especially in a three-way pot. Leading out here with only Ace-high doesn't accomplish much, as I'm likely to get called by any pair or better, and I'd prefer to see if I can get to a cheap showdown or catch an Ace on the turn.

I am in the big blind with King-high on a paired board of 6-9-6. This board is relatively dry, and neither opponent has shown aggression yet. With King-high, I have very little showdown value, but betting into two opponents here would be a pure bluff that is unlikely to succeed. Checking is the most prudent play to see a free turn card and re-evaluate.

Both opponents checked to me on a dry, paired board. A small continuation bet of 1/3 pot is very likely to fold out their unimproved overcards and take down the pot right here. Even if called, my Ace-high gives me decent equity, but the primary goal is to capitalize on the fold equity.

On a paired board of 6-9-6, my Ace-high is currently a marginal hand. While Pro's small continuation bet of 250 into a 750 pot often represents a wide range, the fact that we are three-handed increases the likelihood that someone has connected with the board or holds a pocket pair. Furthermore, I have the Big Blind (Flashlight) still left to act behind me, and being out of position for the rest of the hand makes it difficult to realize my equity. With only 150 hands in the tournament and my stack being the shortest, I prefer to fold and preserve my chips for a higher-equity situation rather than floating with Ace-high in a multi-way pot.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Ten on a paired board of 6-9-6. The opponent, Pro, has made a small continuation bet of 250 into a pot of 750. With 4:1 pot odds, I have sufficient price to call and see a turn card, hoping to catch a King or Ten, or to re-evaluate based on the opponent's further action. While I have no made hand, the board texture is dry, and I am getting a good price to continue.

I am in the Big Blind with King-high on a paired board of 6-9-6-5. I called a small bet on the flop, and now on the turn, I have no made hand or significant draw. Checking is the most prudent play to see the opponent's action, as I have no incentive to lead out and I want to keep the pot controlled.

With Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw, I have decent equity but little fold equity against a range that called the flop. Checking behind allows me to realize my equity for free, control the pot size, and avoid getting check-raised off my draw. It also takes me to the river where I can evaluate whether to try to win at showdown if I hit my outs or consider a delayed bluff.

I am in the Big Blind with King-high on a board of 6-9-6-5-3. I have no made hand and my King-high is extremely weak. Since the opponent checked the turn, they may have a marginal hand or a missed draw, but betting as a bluff here is unlikely to succeed as it would be difficult to represent a strong hand effectively. Checking is the most prudent play to minimize losses, and if the opponent also checks, I get to reach a showdown for free, even if my hand is likely to lose.

My Ace-high has reasonable showdown value against busted straight draws (like 87 or T8) or overcard floats (like KQ/QJ) that Flashlight might have called with on the flop. If I bet, I am almost certainly only getting called by hands that beat me, such as pairs (9x, 5x, or pocket pairs). There are very few better Ace-highs in his range that I could fold out, so checking behind to realize my showdown value and control the risk is the optimal play.

I am on the button with K9o and a stack of 28 BBs. In a three-handed game, K9o is a strong enough hand to open for a raise from the button to pick up the blinds or play a pot in position. My stack is relatively short compared to the other players, so I need to look for profitable spots to accumulate chips before the tournament ends in 5 hands. I will follow my established pattern of raising to 300.

7-9 offsuit is a weak hand with poor playability, especially when playing out of position against an aggressive button open. With only 5 hands remaining and a healthy stack lead, there is no need to engage in a marginal spot. Folding is the disciplined and strategic choice.

I am in the Big Blind facing a 3x open raise from the Button. My hand is 3c 5h, an extremely weak, disconnected, and offsuit hand. Playing this hand out of position against a pre-flop raiser is an easy way to lose chips, so folding is the straightforward and correct decision here.

I am on the button with 7h2h. This is a very weak hand, but I am in position and the cost to call is only 100 into a pot of 150. With 9-2 offsuit in the Small Blind, the hand has extremely poor playability and equity, even getting 5 to 1 to complete the bet. Given our positional disadvantage and the fact that the Big Blind still acts behind us, folding is the fundamentally sound decision to preserve our stack.

I am holding Ace-Ten offsuit (As Tc) in the Big Blind. Flashlight, the chip leader, has limped from the Button, and the Small Blind has folded. A standard raise to 400 (3x the big blind plus the limp) is a solid play here to put pressure on a speculative limping range and capitalize on my hand strength.

My hand is [2h 7h], which is extremely weak. Calling a raise of 300 into a pot of 550 is mathematically incorrect and unnecessary given my chip stack. I will fold and preserve my stack for a better opportunity with only a few hands left in the tournament.

I am on the Button with AKo, a premium starting hand. Open-raising is the optimal play here to build the pot, take control of the hand, and potentially win the blinds outright. A standard raise size of 3 big blinds (300) gets value from worse hands that might call, such as weaker Aces or Broadways, and defines my range as strong. With Flash being relatively short-stacked, this size easily sets up a profitable call if he decides to 3-bet shove.

I am currently the short stack with 3,050 chips and we are on hand 147 of 150. Pro has raised from the button to 300. Holding King-Six offsuit in the Small Blind is a weak position to defend, especially out of position. While I need to find a spot to improve my standing, calling 250 more with a marginal hand like K6o against a button raise often leads to difficult post-flop situations that could bleed more of my stack. With only three hands remaining after this, I prefer to fold and look for a better spot to commit my chips, such as in the Big Blind or when I have the button.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Ten offsuit, which is a strong hand in a heads-up or three-way pot, especially against a button raise. Pro has raised to 300, and Flash has folded. The pot is 450, and I need to call 200 to see the flop. With an 80bb+ stack, calling is standard and allows me to see a flop with a premium Ace. Raising is an option, but calling maintains a wider range and keeps the pot manageable, although I have the equity to potentially re-raise given the position. I will call to realize equity.

I have flopped top pair with a decent kicker on a board that contains both straight and flush draw possibilities. As the player out of position, checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard and most strategic play, allowing me to gauge their strength while keeping the pot controlled. If they bet, I can evaluate my options based on the size of their wager and the texture of the board, as my hand is currently strong but vulnerable.

I have top pair, top kicker on a somewhat draw-heavy flop with a flush draw and straight possibilities. Flashlight checked to me, making this a clear spot for a continuation bet for value and protection. I want to charge any flush draws, straight draws, and weaker Aces that Flashlight might have defended with in the Big Blind. Betting a bit over half the pot accomplishes this while building the pot for my strong hand.

I have top pair with a Ten kicker on an Ace-high board. While my hand is strong, the board is somewhat wet with flush and straight draws present. The opponent's bet is small, representing a standard continuation bet. Raising here would bloat the pot and potentially isolate me against hands that have me beat (like a set or a better Ace) or force the opponent to fold their bluffs. Calling is the most balanced approach; it allows me to keep the opponent's bluffing range wide, control the size of the pot, and evaluate the turn card safely. I am not ready to commit significant chips beyond a call given the texture.

I hold top pair with an Ace, but my kicker is relatively weak. The board is becoming increasingly wet with the turn card (7d) introducing potential straight and flush draws. Being out of position against the button, checking is the most prudent strategy to control the pot size and gain information about my opponent's hand strength. If the opponent checks behind, I can re-evaluate on the river. If they bet, I will be better positioned to decide if my hand remains strong enough to continue based on the sizing.

With Top Pair, Top Kicker on a very draw-heavy turn board (featuring both heart and diamond flush draws, as well as multiple straight draws), I must continue to bet for value and protection. Giving a free card here would be a mistake, as there are many river cards that could potentially kill my action or beat my hand. Betting around 60% of the pot will effectively charge my opponent's draws and worse Aces to see the river.

I hold top pair with an Ace, and while my ten kicker is not the strongest, it remains competitive. The board has developed draws (flush and straight), so Pro's bet of 800 could be a value bet with a better ace or a semi-bluff with a draw. Given my stack size and position, calling is the most balanced move to control the pot size while keeping his bluffing range wide. I am not ready to fold a strong Ace, nor do I want to bloat the pot against a potentially superior hand with a raise.

I am in the Big Blind and have reached the river with top pair (Aces) and a ten kicker. The board is [Ah, 4h, 5d, 7d, 2s], which is relatively dry but allows for some straight possibilities (3-6). My opponent (Pro) has been the aggressor throughout the hand, betting both the flop and the turn. As the out-of-position player, checking is the standard play here. Betting would only isolate me against hands that have me beaten (better Aces, sets, or two pairs) and force folds from any bluffs my opponent might have. Checking allows me to reach showdown affordably and potentially induce a final bluff from a missed draw, or simply check-down if the opponent also has a weak hand.

The river brings the 2s, which is a relative blank, completing neither the heart nor diamond flush draws. While any 3 makes a straight (A-2-3-4-5), that is a very narrow part of Flashlight's range given the pre-flop and flop action. Flashlight's river check strongly signals he likely has a one-pair hand, such as a weaker Ace (AQ, AJ, AT), or a busted draw. With Top Pair, Top Kicker, I have a clear value-betting opportunity. Betting around 40-50% of the pot is the perfect size to get crying calls from weaker Aces while allowing us to comfortably fold if we get check-raised.

The board is A♥ 4♥ 5♦ 7♦ 2♠. Pro has been betting consistently throughout the hand, representing at least an Ace or a potential straight (3-6). However, holding A♣ T♠ gives me top pair with a good kicker, which is a strong bluff-catcher. My Ace blocks several combinations of better hands (like A-K or A-Q). Given the pot odds (1,200 to call a 4,150 pot), I need only 22.4% equity to break even, and there are plenty of missed flush draws (hearts and diamonds) in Pro's range that he could be turning into a bluff. Folding would be too tight, and raising provides no value against his range. Therefore, calling is the most balanced and mathematically sound decision.

I am on the button with a pocket pair of sevens (7h7c). With a stack of 3050 chips (approx. 30.5 BB) and being the short stack among the three players at hand 148 of 150, this is a very strong hand to open with in a three-handed game. I will make a standard opening raise of 3x the big blind to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially take down the blinds or extract value from weaker holdings.

I am in the small blind with Jack-Five offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. Defending out of position against a button raise is generally poor strategy unless the hand is particularly strong. Given the limited number of hands remaining in the tournament and my healthy stack size, folding is the most disciplined and prudent play to preserve my position.

With Ace-Jack suited in the Big Blind facing a Button open from a shorter stack, a 3-bet is a strong play to extract value from worse Aces and broadways, and to put pressure on his ~30bb stack. I'll 3-bet to 900, fully prepared to call a jam or play a bloated pot post-flop.

I am holding pocket sevens (7h7c) on the button. This is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. I opened for 300, and Pro (the big stack in the Big Blind) has three-bet me to 900. I have 2750 chips remaining in my stack. At this late stage of the tournament (hand 148 of 150), and being the shortest stack, I cannot afford to play passively. Calling the 3-bet would commit a significant portion of my stack and leave me in a difficult spot on most flops. By four-bet shoving all-in, I maximize my fold equity and ensure that I realize the full equity of my pair if called. I am risking my remaining 2750 chips for a total commitment of 3050.

Facing a 4-bet jam for about 30bb total from the Button, we are getting excellent pot odds, needing roughly 35% equity to call profitably. Given the wide ranges typical in Button vs. Big Blind dynamics, Ace-Jack suited performs well against the opponent's potential 4-bet shoving range, which likely includes mid-to-small pocket pairs, weaker suited Aces, and various broadway combinations. Calling is mathematically sound and presents a good opportunity to eliminate the short stack.

I am on the button with 7h Jc. The pot is 150. I need to call 100 to stay in the hand. Although my hand is not particularly strong, I have good position and the call is small relative to my stack. I will call and see a flop.

9dTd is a very playable suited connector that performs excellently post-flop. Getting excellent pot odds (50 chips to win 250), completing the small blind is a low-risk, high-reward play. It allows us to see a flop cheaply without bloating the pot out of position against the button limper.

In the Big Blind with 9h 2h, the action has come to me limped with both the button and small blind calling the 100-chip blind. Since I am already in the hand and there is no raise, checking to see a free flop is the most prudent play, especially given the late stage of the tournament (Hand 149 of 150).

With 9d Td on a 7s Qh Qd flop, I have no made hand and only weak backdoor straight and flush draws. Betting into two opponents on a paired board where they could easily have a Queen, a 7, or a better pocket pair is unnecessary and risky. Checking to evaluate the action from the Big Blind and the Button is the standard and safest play.

I have a very weak hand with 9-high on a board that features a pair of Queens (7s-Qh-Qd). The pot was limped pre-flop, and the Small Blind has checked. Since I have no equity and no reason to build a pot with this hand, the most logical action is to check and take a free card to the turn.

I have bottom pair on a paired board with two Queens. Both opponents have checked to me. Betting with a weak pair in a three-way pot is unlikely to get value from worse hands and risks inflating the pot against a potential Queen or better kicker. I will check to keep the pot small and see the turn.

With 9d Td on a 7s Qh Qd 3h board, I have completely missed and have zero equity or draws. Although the pot was checked around on the flop, betting here as a pure bluff into two opponents without any equity or blockers is an unnecessary risk. Checking and preparing to fold to any bet is the optimal play.

I have 9-high with a heart flush draw on a paired board in a limped pot. The Small Blind has checked to me on the turn. Since the flop was checked around, it's likely nobody has a strong hand, but with two opponents, betting into them with 9-high is risky. I will check and take the free card to see if I can improve on the river.

I have a pair of 7s on a paired board with two Queens. Both opponents have checked to me on both the flop and the turn, signaling they likely do not have a Queen or a strong hand. Betting here as a value bet is thin because if I am called, I am likely behind. Checking behind is the most prudent play to keep the pot small, minimize risk, and see the river, especially given this is the second to last hand of the tournament.

My Ten-high has no showdown value, but on this coordinated river that brings a flush completing card and two opponents behind me who checked down, attempting a pure bluff is unnecessary and risky. Someone could easily have paired the 7, 4, or 3, or hold a random heart and call. Checking to give up on this small pot is the safest and most optimal play.

On the river, the board has completed a heart flush (Qh, 3h, 4h) and is also paired with Queens (Qh, Qd). I hold the 9h, giving me a 9-high flush. The action has been extremely passive, with every player checking on the flop and turn. Pro (the Small Blind) has now checked the river as well. While I have a flush, it is relatively low, and any heart higher than a 9 or any Queen (for trips/full house) would beat me. In a limped pot, ranges are wide, and a higher heart is possible. Betting is unlikely to get called by worse hands, so I will check to see if the button (Flashlight) bets or to simply take the hand to a cheap showdown.

I am on the river in the second to last hand of the tournament. The board is paired with Queens and has checked through to me. My pair of 7s is a bluff-catcher at best, and it is very unlikely that either opponent will call a bet with a hand worse than mine, while they could easily hold a Queen or a better pocket pair that I have no chance of beating. Given the passive action from my opponents throughout the hand, checking behind is the most prudent play to secure a potential split or win at showdown without risking chips unnecessarily.

It's the final hand of the tournament, and I am dealt 6-5 offsuit on the button. This is a very weak starting hand, and there's no reason to risk chips trying to steal or play a marginal spot. The best decision to preserve and maximize my stack size is to simply fold.

I am in the Small Blind with 9c2c. The button (Pro) has folded. The pot is 150 and it is only 50 more for me to call into a 200 total pot, or I could raise. However, 92s is a very weak hand. Even though I have 6300 chips and it's the final hand of the tournament (#150), there's little merit in playing this hand against the Big Blind (Flashlight) who has a massive stack. While the cost to call is small (50), 92s doesn't play well post-flop and I'd rather just end my tournament session without unnecessarily losing more chips with a junk hand.
